### nonchalant — 2013-08-05T18:44:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worst-places-to-live-for-emf.6708/post-91559>

> Over the past two decades, the percentage of families that have children has fallen in most of the country, but nowhere more dramatically than in our largest, densest urban areas. In cities with populations greater than 500,000, the population of children aged 14 and younger actually declined between 2000 and 2010, according to U.S. Census data, with New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, and Detroit experiencing the largest numerical drop. Many urban school districts—such as Chicago, which has 145,000 fewer school-age children than it had a decade ago—have seen enrollments plummet and are busily closing schools. The 14-and-younger population increased in only about one-third of all census-designated places, with the greatest rate of growth occurring in smaller urban areas with fewer than 250,000 residents.

[http://www.city-journal.org/2013/23\_3\_childless-cities.html](http://www.city-journal.org/2013/23_3_childless-cities.html)

When 'they' do double down on plans for a completely microwave RF-based interconnected world (I of T) world supplanted by space-based transmitters then truly we are going to get a thrashing no matter where we might be.

Our best recourse for now is to follow the protocols, and harden your dwelling to avoid worst-case exposures. For some people this may mean making your abode a **'fortress of solitude**' from EMFs with tons of shielding and Bau Biologie best practices. Others may also want to burrow underground (earth contact homes) to increase protection (but come up into the sunlight often enough to get its health effects, too).

Unfortunately, I've noticed a strong trend in my metro area of young adults (millenials-- our future generation of leaders and innovators supposedly) flocking to dense urban areas to live in converted loft apartments in old office buildings, or brand new multi-family high rises. What a nightmare-- they will be fully exposed to 5G at full power directly beamed at them because they live at higher elevations.

Stupid is as stupid does.

### saben — 2013-04-10T22:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-about-hcg-and-jacks-protocols.6718/post-83654>

I'm thinking about doing HCG. I have Hashimoto's( diagnosed this fall) and have been using the Mitochondrial prescription, non-flouridated water, mitigating EMF's, using bluelight blocking glasses at night, taking DHEA, thyroid medication, and progesterone.(I'm checking the Magnetico sleep pads and Schumann generators as well). My sleep in much improved-actually dreaming and sleeping deeply and I feel really hydrated after drinking only non-flouridated water for about 2 weeks now. My energy level still is not where I want it to be, but deeper sleep and DHEA is helping. I did a round of HCG about 2 years ago and lost weight, but I was tired most of the round and it was really difficult. I gained the weight back. I think I can keep the weight off this time following Jack's prescriptions, CT, mitigating nighttime light and EMF. But, I'm concerned with my energy level on HCG and really want to be successful.
Can I continue with the CoenzymeQ10(LEF SuperUbiquinal) and D-Ribose while on HCG( I think the rest of Mitochondrial RX supplements are ok)
Can I eat breakfast of 2 portions of protein to stick to circadian eating, then a protein and vegie for dinner instead of skipping breakfast(500 calories total)?
Can I eat oysters, scallops, clams or mussels as part of the protein choices( with shrimp, crab, lobster, whitefish) to stay Epi-paleo?
If I'm tired, meaning I again don't get the energy surge most get on HCG, what can I do?

Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-10T13:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-about-hcg-and-jacks-protocols.6718/post-171555>

Well........the ratio's are 100% affected by the environment of where you are.......and that BUN/creatinine ratio needs to be below 10:1. while also having a goo H/H........people who struggle with both ratio's tend to do horrible with HCG longer term.........because they can't amplify signals in cells using UV light. Response and control mechanisms are linked but not always coupled properly in nature. Many people are fundamentally using HCG to focus light to try to decode messages their cells are not getting. Messages from nature to our cells must be delivered optimally. This is why our genome is designed to respond to environmental signals, not control them. In nature atoms are coupled to light to control responses. To believe in omnipontent power of genes supports lazy comfort and ignorance in people. When the message from outside in is jumbled, inflammation and poor redox results and diseases manifest. The environment's message is given, and it's never designed to be the same in this framework. Just as person will never give a message that everybody will agree with. So the receiver has to be able to decipher the code. I know that even my most faithful readers will never agree 100% with what I say. Nor should they because their environmental canvas is not the same as my own. The easiest way to control people to be passive and obedient is to strictly limit the spectrum of acceptable opinion, but allow very lively debate within that spectrum. I have a sense this is why nature controls the light spectrum in us to maintain order of our proteins. My sense in you is HCG will lead to more heartache because your environmental canvas is the real problem.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-30-is-really-45.6721/post-83784>

This morning a friend of mine - posted about a friend of her's who's 6yo when into heart failure yesterday - they're calling it cardiac arrest. this little boy is now in the ICU waiting for a diagnosis... the first thing I thought of was EMF influences...

### Dextery — 2013-04-11T22:31:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-30-is-really-45.6721/post-83789>

I have a friend whose 16 year old son was just diagnosed with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.
[http://www.medicinenet.com/wolff-parkinson-white\_syndrome/article.htm](http://www.medicinenet.com/wolff-parkinson-white_syndrome/article.htm)

Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome is a condition characterized by abnormal electrical pathways in the heart that cause a disruption of the heart's normal rhythm (arrhythmia).

The heartbeat is controlled by electrical signals that move through the heart in a highly coordinated way. A specialized cluster of cells called the atrioventricular node conducts electrical impulses from the heart's upper chambers (the atria) to the lower chambers (the ventricles). Impulses move through the atrioventricular node during each heartbeat, stimulating the ventricles to contract slightly later than the atria.

When I suggested that EMFs could play a role in the electrical beat of his sons heart, I was met with a blank stare. I asked if he had Wifi in his house and wifi TV. Sure enough...for many years. So far beyond belief and comprehension.

So hard to convince people about EMFs...but I keep trying.

### Rachelle — 2013-04-11T22:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-30-is-really-45.6721/post-83791>

Wow....people need to wise up for their children's sake. ---damnit

### caroline — 2013-04-11T23:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-30-is-really-45.6721/post-83800>

I don't understand people's reluctance ...this isn't exactly new information ...there has been rumbling for years about all of this - and when it is your child - I would turn over every possible stone. These are very heartbreaking stories....

### cantweight — 2013-04-22T17:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-30-is-really-45.6721/post-84624>

One thing I have noticed over and over again especially when I was living inEMF hell is that parents are too tired/stressed to help themselves and their children. I fought tooth and nail to get my health back so I could be strong enough to help my children. I was not going to chance them growing up without a mother or without some vibrancy in their life.

Once I began to feel better and starting telling people to wake up and help their kids and themselves I lost a ton of "friends". All these moms were exhausted and strife and inconvenience were not options. Even when they could see the true cost of their decisions as people were constantly becoming ill in our area. It was heartbreaking to watch and I am glad I was able to leave that.

# Thread: Listen to Your Brain's Own Rhythms

**Subforum:** The Cave

### Huck — 2013-04-12T15:20:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/listen-to-your-brains-own-rhythms.6742/post-83882>

I bet EMF screws this up big time.

> Sleep is where our brains lay down long-term memories. Sounds tuned to our brain's own rhythms might improve the process, suggests one small but promising sleep study.
>
> Looking for better sleep? Lose the white-noise machine and listen to your brain's own rhythms, suggests a study published Thursday.
>
> **During deep sleep, the brain's electrical patterns follow a slow oscillating rhythm**, notes a research team headed by Jan Born of the University of Tubingen in Germany. Some sleep researchers have induced these rhythms in rats with "pathological" sleep patterns, using electrical stimulation in a bid to make them sleep better.

[more](http://www.usatoday.com/story/tech/sciencefair/2013/04/11/sleep-memories-sounds/2075009/)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-04-13T15:30:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-the-program-this-excited-is-vermonter-is-ready-for-change.6744/post-83989>

Are you successful in living your life? Maybe you need to consider becoming more intentional. You don't fall into success it comes with intentional action. Life is not a lottery ticket, where it all just happens as you desire. We must plan to do and be intentional in our actions to get what we want. Living with intention means you are artists who lives in a deliberate fashion. It leads your "why" to discover your WOW. If time and money were non factors in your life, ask yourself what would you be doing. Whatever you answer is what you owe yourself to do in your life. That one question will help you discover your life's why? …......exactly why life is worth living and reward you with countless wow! Moments that enrich your life experience in very meaningful ways. There can be nothing more meaningful than living out the purpose of our life and pursuing it passionately. Simply put this is a state of being intentional. Today consider becoming more like this. It implies purpose, focus, commitment, discipline, planning and action.
Never settle for what might have been when you can create what you want to be. When you think this way what you want will become what you shall be. ! Practice life with intention! And welcome!

# Thread: Welcome to the Jack Kruse Community Cookbook!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/>
**Subforum:** Community Cookbook
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Support — 2013-04-12T20:58:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-83925>

Hi Everyone!

We've got a fun community project in the works, and we need YOU to make it awesome! We are going to build a community cookbook of epi-paleo recipes (woo hoo!), and we're hoping you're jonesing to chip in.

We've set up a special forum on the site for you to submit your recipes (by season, of course!). Please follow Dr. Kruse's guidelines for epi-paleo seasonal eating. Pictures of your finished dishes are welcome, but no links, please! Pictures outside are best!

Once we've compiled the submissions, we will have the community vote on their favorite recipe to feature for each season. The winners will receive a free copy of the new cookbook!

[Seasonal guidelines](http://www.epicurious.com/articlesguides/seasonalcooking/farmtotable/seasonalingredientmap)

Hurry and submit your favorite epi-paleo recipes! All submissions are due by April 30, 2013. (We'll leave the forum open, however, so you guys can keep submitting to your heart's content until the apocalypse!)

See you online!

Sandy & Dr. Kruse

### Coldsnow — 2017-05-30T08:34:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-215075>

Sandy, just curious if that cookbook on sauces ever came to fruition from the Q&A _in 2012?

a great greensauce I could add would be:

1 avocado
1 cup cilantro
sea salt
serrano chili and/or habanero_
avocado oil and/or organic olive oil
lime juice
zest the peel of lime
lots of ginger
lots of turmeric
black pepper
structured water

blend till a thick consistency - absolutely amazing on Salmon
the lime and turmeric work great with the salmon fats

cheers

### Hannah Bagrich — 2020-04-02T14:37:56+0300

### Christina Gagnon — 2020-05-15T17:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-289583>

Does anyone have a good homemade sugarless bacon recipe?

### Marko Pollo — 2020-05-16T20:28:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-289613>

Hey Christina!
I buy a pork belly from Costco. I know, factory farmed, but pork is super hard to find.
I cut it into 4 equal pieces, rub it with a heaping tablespoon of Redmond kosher salt. Wrap it in parchment paper and refrigerate for a week. Freeze 3 and enjoy the other. You can smoke it a bit if you want. I slice it as I need it. Way easier to slice when cold and salted.

### Christina Gagnon — 2020-05-17T16:53:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-289629>

[@Marco](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1453/) Pollo Thank you so much for your recipe - I'm going to give it a try because it sounds simple and healthy (and delicious).

### Marko Pollo — 2020-05-17T17:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/welcome-to-the-jack-kruse-community-cookbook.6751/post-289631>

I also like to buy large packs of t bones or New York steaks, depending on the marbling. I salt them and age them for a week before freezing. They get firm and delicious. Makes it easy to slice them in half into thinner steaks that I prefer. Wrap them in parchment and put into gallon freezer bags. If you lose power, they aren’t as fragile and will not cause you worry. Another thing I’ve been doing is pressure canning Mitomeats or Keto cans. Can use smoked brisket, pork belly, or steaks. My wife, I believe secretly chuckled at me for this. Now there are food shortages, she thinks I’m kind of smart. Too bad I’m a million miles from our pantry. Decided to stay in the sun over hunkering down at home. Will find out next week what is left. Good luck with your beef!

# Thread: Face Dunking Confusion...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MeghanK — 2013-04-13T22:13:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84004>

Do you keep dunking your face until you get your skin to 50-55 degrees? Is it suppose to feel like a bad "brain freeze".... I don't have a thermometer yet... maybe I had the water too cold?

### Dextery — 2013-04-14T01:27:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84006>

In the beginning you do what you can stand. The colder the water is the better. Initially don't worry about skin temp...just do what you can do. Pretty soon it will be easier and you can graduate to full body bath in water you can stand. Get a swimming pool thermometer for the bath water. Stand in the water and then decide to sit down and then sit down. You will gasp. But after a min or two you will experience a strange warming feeling in the low extremities even though the water is cold. Just do it.

### caroline — 2013-04-14T02:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84007>

Great advice Dex - it really does work - you just have to decide to do it and then do it!

### Raiken3712 — 2013-04-14T02:18:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84008>

Anything below 98.6 has some benefit. I think someone said UCP activates at 65 or lower, but I'm not sure of where they got that number. Hypothermics.com Ray Cronise site has good information on Cold anyone doing CT might want to check out. I'm almost done reading all his blogs and he has some good stuff. He talks quite a bit about water temperatures at 80 or below being good and air temps of 60 or lower. Water works better when you exercise because it makes you lose heat faster so swimming around in an 80 degree pool can be quite beneficial. I tend to think going lower than 80 probably burns more calories and has a more profound effect, but he does make a good case for 80 doing plenty with the general recommendation that you swim or do activity while in the 80 degree water.

He does think vegan diets are best for health, but that's from his experience. Apparently as part of N1 experimenting hes used those diets and had the best health markers with them in comparison to when he was eating animal foods. Hes not a vegan as he does eat meat, but he has done some decent sized N1s with vegan.

I asked him if perhaps he was in the 20% of people who get inflammation from animal fats and thus might be the reason he does so well on a vegan diet. I haven't gotten an answer on that quite yet. It was in one of the comment threads and he may not have seen the question yet.

For what I'm referring to on the animal fats and inflammation listen to the Dr. Steven Gundry podcast.

[http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2013/01/35-dr-steven-gundry-high-fat-diets-good-vs-bad/](http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2013/01/35-dr-steven-gundry-high-fat-diets-good-vs-bad/)

Basically he is say larger amounts of animal saturated fats that occur in many people's Paleo can lead to inflammation for some people which amounts to around 20%. Those with an ApoE gene type test of 3/4 or 4/4 may need to keep saturated fats from animals lower.

That would mean an Epi-Paleo diet with less meat, but still keeping plenty of fish and seafood could still be ideal. Saturated fats from coconut oil, red palm oil, and other non-animal sources would still be fine. I don't know enough on the issue, but it perhaps the 20% of people like this had ancestors that were eating mostly fish/seafood with very little meat and the rest from plant foods.

Since I haven't read much on it this is only guessing...if anyone has any data on this I would love to read it.

\*On a side note Jack Kruse drinks Black Tea?....Does Whole Market have less Fluoride or is just the fact that getting plenty of Iodine/Epi-Paleo as Jack does negates the Fluoride and other negative factors(I've heard tannins are higher which may be a negative...or at least I think I read that..perhaps it was something else) Is it a taste thing with black tea? I've heard White Tea has more antioxidants and less Fluoride/Tannin\*

### freesia — 2013-04-14T07:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84042>

> PS we have a tradition of jumping in super-quick here...in, sit down, lie back in one quick move...it puts a small tidal wave over the side of the bath...but ya don't feel a thing!

I need to say though - now I don't have issues. Low body temp in the beginning (that's a year ago now) meant after-drop and warm up time was pretty challenging. Now, afterwards I just feel really good...Pain free, clear headed, fit and strong....oh...and warm lol.

### Inger — 2013-04-14T08:15:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84045>

> [Raiken3712 said:](/goto/post?id=84008)
>
> He does think vegan diets are best for health, but that's from his experience. Apparently as part of N1 experimenting hes used those diets and had the best health markers with them in comparison to when he was eating animal foods. Hes not a vegan as he does eat meat, but he has done some decent sized N1s with vegan.
>
> I asked him if perhaps he was in the 20% of people who get inflammation from animal fats and thus might be the reason he does so well on a vegan diet. I haven't gotten an answer on that quite yet. It was in one of the comment threads and he may not have seen the question yet.
>
> For what I'm referring to on the animal fats and inflammation listen to the Dr. Steven Gundry podcast.
>
> [http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2013/01/35-dr-steven-gundry-high-fat-diets-good-vs-bad/](http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2013/01/35-dr-steven-gundry-high-fat-diets-good-vs-bad/)
>
> Basically he is say larger amounts of animal saturated fats that occur in many people's Paleo can lead to inflammation for some people which amounts to around 20%. Those with an ApoE gene type test of 3/4 or 4/4 may need to keep saturated fats from animals lower.
>
> That would mean an Epi-Paleo diet with less meat, but still keeping plenty of fish and seafood could still be ideal. Saturated fats from coconut oil, red palm oil, and other non-animal sources would still be fine. I don't know enough on the issue, but it perhaps the 20% of people like this had ancestors that were eating mostly fish/seafood with very little meat and the rest from plant foods.
>
> Since I haven't read much on it this is only guessing...if anyone has any data on this I would love to read it.

Raiken,
I wonder if this has to do with the fat being cooked / heated (maybe the heated protein too.. I feel my energy get heavier when I eat cooked red meat, that is why I almost never do it - with fish it is different), I really wonder. Raw animal fats fits me so much better than cooked. We all know something happends to fat when heated... and it is not a good thing. I know many who say they feel awesome on raw animal fats but get so sick when they eat cooked animal fats. It is not that I get sick when eating cooked fats, I sometimes do eat them, like organic butter (not raw) but I always prefer raw if I can get it.

I read somewhere raw animal fats helps inflammation, the cooked variant does the opposite... I would not call it bullshit so far, just from my own N=1

Maybe this issue is why he feels better on vegan? It would be interesting if he could do a N=1 with raw animal fats only.. it really would. I myself is just learning and I can only speak for how I feel but it would be so cool if many would experiment and tell if there was any difference.

### PrimalPam — 2013-04-14T23:54:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84093>

I did the face dunk once and hated it. I know, it's not about liking it vs. not liking it...I just hated it. So I figured, CT = not for me.

I was bored that day (same day as 1st hated face dunk), so later that day, I took my entire icemaker and dumped the contents into my tub and filled with water. Got out a meat thermometer, and the water was about 65F. I got in slowly and stayed in about 30 minutes, finding it so much easier than the face dunk.

Ever since that first tub, I've been hooked, now getting the water to 50F for 45 mins to 1 hour. I've never done a facedunk since.

### cantweight — 2013-04-15T19:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-confusion.6767/post-84163>

I was similar...face dunks were annoying. First couple tubs took a bit of psyching up and gasping, now I look forward to my soak. It is quiet and I feel like I am putting out any fires in my bod...muscular joint, whatever. I even add some peppermint Dr Bronners soap for bubbles.

My kids are funny, I gave them permission to yell "balls" really loud as they get in. They think its so funny they just CT so they can yell balls...lol!

### Dextery — 2013-04-14T04:48:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84026>

You don't see Dr K juicing because he doesn't do it. Juicing veggies is like dumping a lot of carbs into the gut that will immediately spike the blood sugar.

Remember, there are no essential carbohydrates necessary for survival and optimal. Not mentioned in any of the RX prescriptions at all that I can remember.

About the only juicing I have seen is a lady that had access to a fish processing plant and got all the fish heads she needed for nothing and took them home and juiced them with some added stevia. Brain powder juicing epi-paleo style.

### Raiken3712 — 2013-04-14T18:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84067>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=84066)
>
> I actually have never juiced.... just heard "Drew Canole" interviewed on a million podcasts and all the "DETOX" diets seem to be juice fasts etc.... so just wondering...
> I am guessing you feel the same way about smoothies... it just seems like a quick way to spike glucose and sugar! Good, I don't need to waste money on a Vitamix

I have heard that blending the vegetables and fruits together increases blood sugar faster....but I'm thinking every once in awhile with the right blend of fruits and vegetables it wouldn't be too bad as long as you don't have huge quantities.

A small amount of berries with some kale and some other greens. I figure Jack probably doesn't do them and wouldn't advise them, but to me it sounds like it could be a perfectly healthy choice. I guess blood sugar testing would be the best way to to tell if a smoothie is good for you.

That being said if you don't already have a good blender, and aren't sure what impact it would have on your blood sugar there is no point in buying one to try it out. Also if your drinking one right before a workout and/or CT than that would make a difference as well. I think I remember reading that things are more bioavailable when blended, and you do get more concentrated nutrition, but whether that is worth it would be dependent on that blood sugar to a large extent.

At least that's my thoughts on it.

### fitness@home — 2013-04-14T20:35:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84075>

Although I don't do juicing myself, my husband has juiced periodically over the past six months. He watched the movie "Fat, Sick and Nearly Dead" and decided to give it a try. In between he usually eats a low carb diet.

He went out and bought a Breville juicer and really surprised me by sticking to it. Only juices vegetables...didn't like the sweetness when adding fruit.

Long story short is that he is 30 lbs lighter now. Went from 240 to 210.

Another thing he learned from doing this is that he is very sensitive to dairy... He did not drink milk for 3-4 months. After drinking a glass of milk again he couldn't ignore his body's reaction (lots of phlegm). Very rarely drinks milk now.

### Martin — 2013-04-14T21:07:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84077>

I like juices.

I like the idea of concentrated extraction of fruits and veggies, and a large helping of it just has to be good, right? After all, it's fruits and veggies?

My mom was big on them. We have a big Vitamix. Lots of homegrown stuff to juice.

I was astounded to learn about how the fructose just goes nuts on your blood glucose. A glass of OJ never looked the same after that. Green juices? Prolly okay, but not worth the trouble, and taste, to me.

But what would have my long-lost ancestors done? Gather food and squish it between rocks to greedily gulp the juices? I doubt it, but maybe.

I have chosen to avoid juice, and miss it dearly.

### Raiken3712 — 2013-04-14T21:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84084>

> [Martin said:](/goto/post?id=84077)
>
> I like juices.
>
> I like the idea of concentrated extraction of fruits and veggies, and a large helping of it just has to be good, right? After all, it's fruits and veggies?
>
> My mom was big on them. We have a big Vitamix. Lots of homegrown stuff to juice.
>
> I was astounded to learn about how the fructose just goes nuts on your blood glucose. A glass of OJ never looked the same after that. Green juices? Prolly okay, but not worth the trouble, and taste, to me.
>
> But what would have my long-lost ancestors done? Gather food and squish it between rocks to greedily gulp the juices? I doubt it, but maybe.
>
> I have chosen to avoid juice, and miss it dearly.

I don't think Fructose in juice or anything else has anything to do with making blood glucose go nuts as they are two different kinds of sugars. Fructose in excess can overtax the liver and if not used for energy or to make Glycogen will end up as fat in the liver or elsewhere. Fruit juice spikes blood glucose because it contains a good amount of glucose which is certainly not good. Yeah green juices that include the fiber sound like they could be ok for some people, but I'm not sure I could get into drinking them either. Where my blood glucose and insulin sensitivity are now its probably not a good idea for me anyway.

My best guess is green juices even with included fiber probably aren't good for anyone who isn't Leptin/Insulin sensitive. Perhaps it would be ok for someone doing a workout, but Jack has said before that we shouldn't be exercising much if at all while we are still LR.

### beach bunny — 2013-04-15T05:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-84124>

I used to make green juices in the past quite a bit. My favourite was cucumber, spinach and fennel (weird I know haha). They were recommended to me when I was too weak and sick to digest food (b/c of gut issues and inability to properly chew raw foods to extract nutrients due to TMJ issues).

I also juiced carrots occasionally when I needed a sugar boost and added fish oil to it to stimulate bile production in the liver.

Some strongly advocate juicing for detox (like the Gerson Therapy). You basically drink tons of fresh vegetable juice and do coffee enemas.
I feel they benefit some in the short term, but in the long term they cannot provide the right nutrient density required to stay healthy in this society.

I honestly feel like it hugely depends on the person's environment and their health status; If it is pristine and the body is not bombarded with stressors (chemical, EMF, infectious, metals, work, etc etc) then I would imagine a person can thrive on most natural foods (not artificial processes junk of course).

### beach bunny — 2013-06-28T16:31:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing.6769/post-89135>

> [nuttmegs17 said:](/goto/post?id=84205)
>
> Just be aware that juicing things like spinach kale etc can cause or contribute to thyroid issues. People don't really think about goiter type foods but massive dosing of green juicing can really negatively affect you if you go overboard...a few a here or there doesn't hurt but sometimes people think "if a little of something is good for you then a lot must be great!" and in this case that is not true...

So glad you pointed this out nuttmegs! I had no idea about this thyroid suppressor relationship:
Goiter\[edit\]
Cruciferous vegetables can potentially be goitrogenic (inducing goiter formation). They contain enzymes that interfere with the formation of thyroid hormone in people with iodine deficiency.\[8\]\[9\] Cooking for 30 minutes significantly reduces the amount of goitrogens and nitriles. At high intake of crucifers, the goitrogens inhibit the incorporation of iodine into thyroid hormone and also the transfer of iodine into milk by the mammary gland.\[10\] (from Wikipedia)

Here is a video that clears up the context (it is really an issue for those with **iodine deficiency** !)
[http://renegadehealth.com/blog/2009/08/07/the-real-effect-of-raw-cruciferous-vegetables-on-the-thyroid](http://renegadehealth.com/blog/2009/08/07/the-real-effect-of-raw-cruciferous-vegetables-on-the-thyroid)

### Martha Ray — 2020-01-31T22:17:09+0200

Green veggie juicing limiting the higher sugar foods like carrots and beets helped me when I was recovering from cancer treatment and a resulting blood clot. All may fall into the 'Slipping from the Pond' theory of food as not as important as light or as the the microbiome we got from our mothers or microbiome we get from our environment and others around us.
**Nourish Vermont 2018 | Jeff Leach | Slipping from the Pond,

**Of course that is there and we are where ever we are in the world. Environment must matter as do many other factors plus our light, water and magnetism and perhaps the most important is what we believe and the mind body connection.

I do however believe there are absolute truths but there are many other factors as well and they are often fluctuating ones adding to the complexity....

I went to the Omega Juicer site today for a Labor Day Sale but ha cleaning brushes not even discounted and I think my Brave Browser did not allow my post anyway. Thought I'd share here.... I looked up the current model of my 8006 juicer.
[https://omegajuicers.com/collection...-ultimate-masticating-nutrition-system-juicer](https://omegajuicers.com/collections/quiz-greens/products/j8006hds-omega-ultimate-masticating-nutrition-system-juicer)

II just tried to post this review on my 8006 Omega Juicer .. New one, J**8006**HDS Ultimate Juicer and Nutrition System is still $299.95
\---------------------------------------------
"I love juicing and my older 8006 but learned the hard way to do juicing correctly with mostly low -glycemic fruits and veggies and not go overboard. Get educated about nutrition and juice for health not gluttony entertainment. The 8006 is as good as any juicer and better than most for a price that won't break the bank! I am using mine weekly or monthly for 11 years and those early years were infusions of life when I was at death's door. But I did go on to experience some juicing dangers ...like Oxalate, and Candida rashes from too much sugar. I suggest if someone is new to juicing to start low and go slow. Juicing is a a sugar hit even with the lowest glycemic lowest carb green veggies. Learn some great tips at CureZone...google "Juicing CureZone' and look for a result from CureZone.org One link to short CureZone article, as of 9 6 20 is [https://www.curezone.org/faq/q.asp?a=12,109,1076&q=42](https://www.curezone.org/faq/q.asp?a=12,109,1076&q=42)

I juice less now 4-6 oz 1 or 2 times a week and I juice and drink at once in 'seconds' not 'minutes'. Clean up later. BTW this juicer has a been easy to clean and as I mentioned I still love it after 11 years!!! Omega builds these with quality...a rare find today.

For those seeking health: I had a concern about another review posted here ........that _**'No colds for Me'**_ recipe... of carrots, apples, oranges etc. being way too much of a sugar hit for most people.... drinking too much...too often of that recipe in my opinion would promote cancer, candida and or diabetes. I also think the best advice encourages to use all real unprocessed, fresh, organic, seasonal and local ingredients. If drinking this in the wintertime up north one would be setting themselves up for a body shocking mismatch per Dr. Jack Kruse. Dr. Kruse is one cutting edge health educator way ahead of his time.

I am serious, careful and detailed because I've made a lot of mistakes recovering my health....and because of the things I've suffered. I'm not meaning to be a 'kill joy' with all my details and will share that our family enjoys making a desert of organic frozen bananas alternated with frozen berries in our juicer with the sorbet attachment....nothing else...no sugar added This makes a great occasional treat!
By grace our bodies can recover from a shock from time to time.
Blessed is the man that condemns not himself for the things he allows. Romans 14:22"

# Thread: Squatting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 110
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2013-04-15T00:37:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84102>

Has anyone else been doing this regularly? I've been squatting daily since the Power Squat post, and it has been doing wonders for my lower spine and also for my knees and feet. I've gotten more limber with time. I also twist left and right while squatting, pushing on the opposite knee, to enhance the stretch of the various muscles and ligaments.

Using a stool while on the potty is fine, but I have found that it is much better (for my back) to do a real squat. So I squat while gardening, cleaning, etc.

[http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-17-the-power-squat/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-17-the-power-squat/)

### endless — 2013-04-15T00:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84109>

I've always been a squatter....I find it a much more comfortable to squat than stand or sit for long periods.

### Martin — 2013-04-15T14:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84138>

I find it easy to squat and always have. Must be those lifetimes spent in the rice patty's. DW even brought up the squatty potty thing. Will wonders never cease?!

### Jude — 2013-04-16T02:36:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84190>

Watched the natives in Papua New Guinea squat non stop( this is 40yrs ago!)-no chairs ........chat sessions always meant squatting for the blokes..
They seemed to use the wider leg squat but seem to remember that the ladies did a narrower squat and always with heels down. Learned the art there and have practised the art ever since.

### MeghanK — 2013-04-16T02:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84198>

### Jude — 2013-04-16T03:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-84202>

### baudoman — 2013-11-15T04:31:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-100745>

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=84190)
>
> Watched the natives in Papua New Guinea squat non stop( this is 40yrs ago!)-no chairs ........chat sessions always meant squatting for the blokes..
> They seemed to use the wider leg squat but seem to remember that the ladies did a narrower squat and always with heels down. Learned the art there and have practised the art ever since.

Interesting experience you have there, Jude. Have you read 'The Continuum Concept'?

It's basically about a woman who did something similar and wrote about the natural way of bringing children up.

Do you think there's any hope of us 'civilized' folk ever learning anything from them? I know that there have been at least some improvements in the last 50 years (importance of breastfeeding, attachment parenting, etc) but for sure we're missing the point about the squatting for #2 thing.

### Jude — 2013-11-15T11:49:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-100769>

> [baudoman said:](/goto/post?id=100745)
>
> Interesting experience you have there, Jude. Have you read 'The Continuum Concept'?
>
> It's basically about a woman who did something similar and wrote about the natural way of bringing children up.
>
> Do you think there's any hope of us 'civilized' folk ever learning anything from them? I know that there have been at least some improvements in the last 50 years (importance of breastfeeding, attachment parenting, etc) but for sure we're missing the point about the squatting for #2 thing.

I think I read that or something like it in the 70's......I feel like I know about it though....was a bit of a talking point for us witnessing "the continuum concept" in action, so to speak, the PNG way of demand feeding/constant mother baby attachment vs the European method of designed feeding, restricted mum bub contact.
The mums developed stong neck muscles pretty quickly because they carried their bubs on their backs in a crocheted billum bag with the strap held firmly on mum's head.... Never had a case of engorged breasts, colicky bubs, or screaming babies.

It certainly was an eye opener for me......
Demand feeding hit in the 70's here....watched it work beautifully up there....the lifestyle really complimented it....Mimiced lots of what I saw up there.....wasn't always successful.......tried to enthuse mums here into squatting for birthing ......bit of a lost cause!

Hey, Jude.. (pardon the reference )

Regarding the birthing thing, I'm male and I feel the squatting for birthing thing looks the most natural, but you're right - there's no way anyone in civilized areas would consider it. I know with the advent of birthing centers they are making progress but the majority of women/people do not even consider the benefits of squatting to their pregnancy.. probably because it is not stressed enough by their medical advisors.

### sooperb — 2014-05-24T17:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-128255>

"Both, for me. My heels go right against my hiney with no effort."

Is that with your bottom on your heels feet flat or on your toes with your bottom resting against them? The former is apparently the better position with your weight resting on your the balls of the feet, ever so slightly tipped forward. It's not as easy as you think. There is a facebook page called something like 30 minute squat challenge which suggests that you should try to squat for 30 minutes a day by working up to it over 30 days. I have been trying but I forget during the day usually but always remember to squat while I clean my teeth at night, trying to extend the time a little. It has made a lot of difference even just doing that, I can squat and raise easier, no discomfort in my back bending over and I feel more flexible.

### Dali Dula — 2014-05-24T19:12:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-128270>

Over the past few years I have been distilling human movement into the most efficient and beneficial movements. The top two are walking and squatting. Squatting is the miracle most of western civilization is missing. All desk bound people should slide off their chairs once an hour and do a two minute squat. Heels down ass to ankles. Anytime I am experiencing fatigue from repetitive motion I drop into a squat and am instantly reinvigorated. Go for a walk and drop into squat every ten minutes or so. Observe the world from a squat. There's a lot going on.

I'll say this blogpost was the start of the healing of my long-term plantar faciitis. [http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-17-the-power-squat/](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-17-the-power-squat/)
Paleo stopped the progression of the disease, and helped me regain a slight ability to use my feet without special shoes, but this blog was the beginning of my cure. Yes, water and seafood helped tremendously, but squatting was just as important. Now I have to be careful to grab some shoes before I leave, or I'll show up to the restaurant or store barefoot. \[Yes, I went into the restaurant anyway. DH was mortified.  \]

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-23T06:15:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135252>

spinal stability first step
watch the infant, all infants go thru a process at differents tages of the first year of infancy
this is the installation of movement "software" that is required for functional ideal in movement in adulthood
installation is complete at 13 months in a health CNS

the stability sequence is saggital, ipsilateral, contralateral.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

note that this sequence is not dependent on "learning" and is a built in sequence in the CNS, no matter where the infant grows up. this shows us it is the only "true" movement model. anything taugfht ie pilates, crossfit "technique" etc is all fabricated.
full squat is 12months, and is dependent on all the prerequisite stability postures. the prerequisite postures are based on spinal alignment and stability to achieve maximal length of spine iwth no folding ie laterally, forward flexion, extension. this is dependent on proper activation of deep system stability for diaphragmatic function. functional ideal is deconditioned the moment shoes are put on the feet, or one is "taught" that sitting in a chair, rather than squatting, is "proper"
If one needs to screw the legs outward or turn the feet out it shows a lack of deep system stability, and a subsequent hyperactivity of parts of the motor system as a compensatory "brace"
one can achieve full squat easily through use of DNS deep system stability exercises, but if squat is terrible, one must progress through the infantile sequence first

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-23T06:16:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135253>

note in the adult shown in the posters the ability to maintain length of spine no matter the position of the hips

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-23T06:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135254>

this is installed at 3 months, where the baby is supine, legs up
the trunk becomes the "punctum fixum" or fixed point around which the hip knows itself.
the hip can only function ideally when its frame of reference is stable

### ssj3 — 2014-07-24T11:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135420>

I am very much looking forward to meeting the people on this forum and the great discussions that will be had.... personally I try not to let my beliefs, prejudices and biases from identifying tools/systems/methods as the perfect approach in certain situations when they would otherwise be discarded. For example: -FMS may be crap, but it can be a quick and simple way to confirm my observations and identify the energy leak (i.e. tight hamstrings, hip flexors, glutes not firing, etc.); - having heels on plates to allow someone to squat to depth while they work on improving ankle mobility; - consuming carbs at certain points in the season.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-24T12:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135422>

> [ssj3 said:](/goto/post?id=135420)
>
> I am very much looking forward to meeting the people on this forum and the great discussions that will be had.... personally I try not to let my beliefs, prejudices and biases from identifying tools/systems/methods as the perfect approach in certain situations when they would otherwise be discarded. For example: -FMS may be crap, but it can be a quick and simple way to confirm my observations and identify the energy leak (i.e. tight hamstrings, hip flexors, glutes not firing, etc.); - having heels on plates to allow someone to squat to depth while they work on improving ankle mobility; - consuming carbs at certain points in the season.

FMS is good for trainers etc but we need to look at why a hamstring might be tight etc, why a glut is not firing
I just see it as very oversimplified given my training i suppose.
Squatting to depth in my opinion should be performer correctly before load is applied. If heels are on plates it throws the posture up and forward and thus is more of a hinderance in achieving full squat ideal in my experience. My experience shows me that this is a case of tryinh to get from A to Z when the aim should be A to B.
In the case of a crap squat, the patients Z is in fact B.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-24T12:08:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135423>

### prAna303 — 2014-07-24T12:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135428>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=135422)
>
> Squatting to depth in my opinion should be performer correctly before load is applied. If heels are on plates it throws the posture up and forward and thus is more of a hinderance in achieving full squat ideal in my experience. My experience shows me that this is a case of tryinh to get from A to Z when the aim should be A to B.
> In the case of a crap squat, the patients Z is in fact B.

Funny thread...

I often see people with serious problems with walking, often linked with pain in feets, knees and back and something that always follow is the ability to squat.

I use it as a test among others, add up their gait and then i often "see" what the problem is. The biggest ones?

\- Long index toe, making your ankle very stiff if you use shoes with heels.
\- Any issue with big toe, if not straight, often stiffness in lower back.
\- Uneaven shoulder height, often locking the spine so that a squat is no longer able to be done.

Of course tons of others, no one is the same but most share the issue of squats.

> Squatting to depth in my opinion should be performer correctly before load is applied.

With you to 100% on this one and even asking, should we ever do squats with weights like we do today. That is like doing a kipping pullup without being a trained gymnast, that most crossfitters are not.

Loose energy - loose flow - gain mass - get stiff - bad protein - steal syndrome

### SeaHorse — 2014-07-25T14:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135525>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=135422)
>
> FMS is good for trainers etc but we need to look at why a hamstring might be tight etc, why a glut is not firing
> I just see it as very oversimplified given my training i suppose.
> Squatting to depth in my opinion should be performer correctly before load is applied. If heels are on plates it throws the posture up and forward and thus is more of a hinderance in achieving full squat ideal in my experience. My experience shows me that this is a case of tryinh to get from A to Z when the aim should be A to B.
> In the case of a crap squat, the patients Z is in fact B.

This A to B stuff brings up a question I have about feet….I have a default to pronating…not terrible and I don't have "flat" feet, meaning I do have an arch, but I noticing that wearing moccasins for most of the summer that my knees are starting to hurt a bit….the question is : is an arch support in a shoe a crutch…a symptom soother, or does it actually work to signal the body to proper use…I'm thinking that I just have to keep attending to this and noticing my feet so that it becomes the new default…but then part of me wonders if I'm throwing everything off balance by not having the support?…can you change anything at a deep level if you are using "support" like this?

Open to all opinions…..

Plus I really want to find a DNS practioner…I need those infant exercises!

### Andrea — 2014-07-25T15:34:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135529>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=135525)
>
> This A to B stuff brings up a question I have about feet….I have a default to pronating…not terrible and I don't have "flat" feet, meaning I do have an arch, but I noticing that wearing moccasins for most of the summer that my knees are starting to hurt a bit….the question is : is an arch support in a shoe a crutch…a symptom soother, or does it actually work to signal the body to proper use…I'm thinking that I just have to keep attending to this and noticing my feet so that it becomes the new default…but then part of me wonders if I'm throwing everything off balance by not having the support?…can you change anything at a deep level if you are using "support" like this?
>
> Open to all opinions…..

Not an answer, but my experience...
I grew up at a place/time where people put shoes on their infants. This may not be the cause, but nevertheless I've always had flat feet with more or less no arches. A bunch of years ago I injured my left foot, with what seemed to be permanent damage to the ligaments. When I stood on it, it sort of floated out and didn't really look much like a foot. Several doctors and specialists urged me to wear sturdy shoes, specially made inserts and to never ever walk barefoot. I did that for years, and I was ok, I suppose.
Well, a few years back I was thinking, wondering if the doctors really, really knew. I started to walk barefoot (with socks) as much as I could. One day I realized that the undefined pain from my hips, knees and feet, which I had taken for granted, was gone. I bought myself a pair of FiveFingers and the last few years I have practically not worn any other shoes.

This spring I had my feet scanned on a "foot scanning machine" and my arches are now considered normal. And both my feet look quite alike, like normal feet.

So, for me, "good" shoes and special inserts were no real help.

### prAna303 — 2014-07-25T16:03:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135531>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=135525)
>
> This A to B stuff brings up a question I have about feet….I have a default to pronating…not terrible and I don't have "flat" feet, meaning I do have an arch, but I noticing that wearing moccasins for most of the summer that my knees are starting to hurt a bit….the question is : is an arch support in a shoe a crutch…a symptom soother, or does it actually work to signal the body to proper use…I'm thinking that I just have to keep attending to this and noticing my feet so that it becomes the new default…but then part of me wonders if I'm throwing everything off balance by not having the support?…can you change anything at a deep level if you are using "support" like this?
>
> Open to all opinions…..
>
> Plus I really want to find a DNS practioner…I need those infant exercises!

When going into shoes without heels many do get some kind of issues with their knees. Many overextend their knees in normal shoes and let that extension go on when going barefeet. Try to learn yourself to not do it, if you do it today.

Overextending the knee also put extra burden on your lower back.

### prAna303 — 2014-07-25T16:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135532>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=135525)
>
> is an arch support in a shoe a crutch…a symptom soother, or does it actually work to signal the body to proper use…I'm thinking that I just have to keep attending to this and noticing my feet so that it becomes the new default…but then part of me wonders if I'm throwing everything off balance by not having the support?…can you change anything at a deep level if you are using "support" like this?

You can put extra strain on the lower back with arch support. I see things like that as a tool to use if you must survive the moment but not in the long run. Toes can also be stressed with extra support.

Listen to what you feel, if you feel sore and tired, rest.

Add on some dancing if possible, good for you body and soul plus it builds strong lowers.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-26T00:34:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135565>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=135525)
>
> This A to B stuff brings up a question I have about feet….I have a default to pronating…not terrible and I don't have "flat" feet, meaning I do have an arch, but I noticing that wearing moccasins for most of the summer that my knees are starting to hurt a bit….the question is : is an arch support in a shoe a crutch…a symptom soother, or does it actually work to signal the body to proper use…I'm thinking that I just have to keep attending to this and noticing my feet so that it becomes the new default…but then part of me wonders if I'm throwing everything off balance by not having the support?…can you change anything at a deep level if you are using "support" like this?
>
> Open to all opinions…..
>
> Plus I really want to find a DNS practioner…I need those infant exercises!

Yes it is a crutch
See here: [http://www.prokineticsaustralia.com.au/](http://www.prokineticsaustralia.com.au/)
This is what we use to address root cause hyperpronation, alongside barefoot walking and running to restore arch integrity
The arch is a spring, a pump and a force tranducer and as such it should not rest on anything. You can hear me discuss it in this podcast [http://www.askbryan.com/thepodcast/65](http://www.askbryan.com/thepodcast/65)

You can use the PCI in first link to achieve best results via proprioceptive change, but its best if you can be assessed by a registered provider
I can assist if you cant find one in your area, just PM me

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-26T00:37:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-135566>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=135541)
>
> I can feel that my toes haven't been doing enough work over the years…much more pressure/callus in the ball of my feet …since last year spending more time barefoot and wearing moccasins have started to wake up my toes.
>
> I'll continue to notice my knees…I remember my Alexander teacher showing me about the proper weight distribution over your bones…no tensing of knee muscles… I think my pain is from the rotation inward.
>
>

Rotation inward predispoosed by ahort first metatarsal, happens in week 10 in the womb, from incomplete unwinding of the limb bud as it pronates and invaginates inward
When it stops early the bug toe is left hanging in tbe air when the talus is in functional ideal. Check out the prokinetics

It is incredibly functional and it looks pretty much like the stabilization exercises you posted.

### Josh — 2014-10-01T18:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-142429>

Perhaps learning to stand properly before learning to squat? Crawl before you walk? In the first kung fu school that I joined, the teacher laughed when we asked how to fight. He said "first learn how to stand up, then we will look at fighting...give it 2-3 years". I am still learning to stand up after 25 plus years. ### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-10-01T23:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-142463>

Well deep system squat is expressed from the cns by 12 months as a normal movement "software" installation during development. What you are all claiming is that you "know" the right way via conceptual understandings, but the only true squat is the infantile squat which isnt taught. Conceptual squats such as those taught in gyms are one of the leading causes of chronic low back and limb dysfunction that come into my clinic.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-10-01T23:31:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-142464>

So in fact the squat is expressed before the walk as a saggital stability program. At 3 months the pattern is mastered in supine, and via change programs during development eventually it is mastered in closed chain limb support

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-10-01T23:32:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-142465>

I would suggest the book lowly origin by jonathon kingdon

### Josh — 2014-10-01T23:34:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-142466>

What you are all claiming is that you "know" the right way via conceptual understandings, but the only true squat is the infantile squat which isnt taught. Have you ever seen someone unwind back to the "natural" squat as Feldenkreis and other developmental systems try to teach. I have not managed it myself. With Arpwave Trainer on my quads, I have been able to do squats that I otherwise cannot do without any damage or pain. It is a subtle and intense override of the circuitry with strong DC current and a carrier wave at 10 kHz to override existing patterns. Still have not decided what I like it for after a year and a half.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-10-01T23:43:28+0300

Yes i have, it is possible but usually only via DNS ie going through the precursor developmental positions first. Usually supine legs up. The key is the diaphragm creating intra abdominal pressure which allows the chest and pelvis to talk, and lumbar erector spinae to relax

One bummer I've identified with my squatting is I've had for like over a year what sounds to be patellofemoral syndrome under the kneecaps. No pain, no noticeable instability, no limited mobility, but a distracting ripping sound upon knee extension like on rising from the squat or leg extension in the foot-hand crawl, more so on the left side (with a longer left leg I suppose that's no surprise if I've had too much asymmetric weight distribution in movement in years past).

CW says "treatment" is avoiding natural movements (ha), NSAIDs and surgery. If any intervention, seems electrotherapy first would be the ticket.

[http://www.paleoosteo.com/when-everyone-is-an-expert-part-4-the-squat/](http://www.paleoosteo.com/when-everyone-is-an-expert-part-4-the-squat/)

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T09:52:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-175098>

The squat is a crucial motor skill milestone and we use it to assess whether a lot of the basic joint range of motion is present for more complex activities (such as running. More complex here = greater instability).

Personally I found the best sources of learning the correct squat to be Lee Saxby (for the fundamentals of the natural resting squat especially the almost always overlooked aspect of proper foot mechanics and optimal loading points of the foot), MovNat (for a fitness system that incorporates the squat and its related motor skill milestones) and Ido Portal's 'Movement Culture' for his fantastic 'squat routine' and '30 minute squat challenge' which is extremely effective in improving the squat for those who are dedicated.

I do not believe a study was ever done to show whether the ability to squat correctly correlates with reduced risk of injury (currently impossible because people don't agree on 'correct squat') but if someone can execute a full deep squat while keeping most of the bodyweight loading over the first metatarsal joint then we know almost for sure that normal joint range of motion exists in all key joints. That is the first prerequisite for injury-free athletic expression, at least from an isolated biomechanical perspective, with the second being the ability to successfully absorb and rebound the ground reaction forces created by the interaction with gravity during contact time in movement (i.e. no good having full joint range of motion if you're only capable of absorbing 1 x your bodyweight in GRF and you are suddenly subjected to 3 x bodyweight. You will still be squashed like a bug).

### Daniel — 2015-12-09T10:21:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-183769>

Squats are really good for strengthening the legs and the core if you do it properly. For those who need form advice, check out the videos of Allan Thrall and Strength Camp on youtube. I think the 3 big movements (squats, deadlifts, bench press) and their accessory exercises (overhead, row, good mornings, etc...) are the ultimate modern strength training... but spicing that with some HIIT circuit training once a week is even better

### Daniel — 2015-12-09T10:21:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/squatting.6779/post-183770>

also read Mark Rippetoe's book and videos

### René Borg — 2015-12-14T13:10:28+0200

I think the 3 big movements (squats, deadlifts, bench press) and their accessory exercises (overhead, row, good mornings, etc...) are the ultimate modern strength training... but spicing that with some HIIT circuit training once a week is even better

Well Alan Thrall now officially has the best scientific definition of 'foot width in squatting' - 'your stance should be as wide as when you take a dump in the woods'.

# Thread: Can we talk Progesterone??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-progesterone.6797/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 73
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2013-04-16T21:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-progesterone.6797/post-84263>

I've worked my way up to 300 mg Pg. I take this orally before bed and it does seem as though it's finally helping my sleep. Strange thing though is that I seem to be having reactions to it that are opposite what CW says should be happening . . . I've never been a normal one

While the Pg initially seemed to be decreasing my appetite and thirst, now it's causing immense cravings. Thankfully I haven't caved yet, but potato chips, french fries and chocolate chip cookies are about all I can think of these days. It's pretty odd considering I haven't had a single food that isn't on the strict AI epi-paleo plan for over a year now and for the last three + years I haven't had any processed / non-paleo food at all! What gives? I thought low Pg caused cravings . . .

I'm grateful that my sex drive seems to be coming back (yay for DH!!), but along with that I feel like I'm an emotional mess . . . depressed doesn't even begin to cut it. Not only am I craving junk food, I'm craving a change in life. I feel compelled to just make a huge jump and do SOMETHING . . . not even sure what! I just have this drive and desire to move, buy a beach house, get a new job, go on a big vacation . . . anything, just SOMETHING. I'm usually pretty rational and logical and think things through, but I'm honestly scared I'm going to dive into something without any planning just because I've got this innate desire to make something happen.

Is this all from the Pg??

Almost forgot - the hot flashes! Again, I thought Pg should be helping this, but I'm having random moments throughout the day where I'm just burning up. It mostly affects my face and upper body. My face gets flushed, bright red and SUPER hot.

Maybe try to worry less and make love more ...... connections with those you love are the most important and the rest will fall into place.

### Shijin13 — 2013-04-16T22:59:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-progesterone.6797/post-84281>

I'm taking a step back here and I'm Looking at the flow of how hormones are made - what do you need to make Progesterone?

LDL + Vitamin A + T3 allows for the conversion of LDL to Pregnenolone. Pregnenolone then if everything is working properly should convert to Progesterone... and you have the whole hormone cascade.

so If we're having problems getting our Pg up - even with supplementation - I think we should be going back and looking at our thyroid. T3 is critical for production of the hormone cascade. so - that leads me to the question of how's your thyroid labs looking?

I know mine are freaking rockin' it right now and I'm making Pregnenolone - but my PG is still in the tank.... and ohhh the hormonal chaos that's on going...

So... I'm thinking a couple of things the HOT your feeling - could be your thyroid kicking on. It could be E-dumping/detoxing. It could be E-dominance fighting the Pg.

I understand your desire to make a change. This is the longest I've lived in one place (13yrs) prior to that I've moved every 1-2years, even growing up. Change is a good thing... you've just got to figure out how to make it work towards your advantage and leverage it to make the most out of life!!

I am wondering if I am lacking the enzymes responsible for the conversion to cortisol? and how do I figure that out?

I am just reading in my notes ....High Testosterone may be due to low tissue response or other hormone imbalance - eg. low thyroid or low cortisol ...... symptoms consistent with low thyroid.

Initially I think JK thought I might be able to improve my sluggish thyroid on my own with C/T etc. the numbers are all going in the right direction - all be it slowly - except for the testosterone.

### tweety — 2013-04-17T01:27:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-progesterone.6797/post-84300>

The longer I was on Progesterone, I noticed that right before my period I started feeling more and more depressed. Initially I felt much calmer and so much reduced PMS irritabilty that I knew I was soaking up the P. But towards the end I really started gaining weight in weird ways and feeling depressed, hating everything (in this last way reverting back to pre-progesterone days).

I have always felt random impulses to move across the country, buy/sell a house/ get a dog, change cars... paint... whatever. I just needed change. For a while, in the good moments of progesterone usage, this calmed down and I was so happy. Then it came back. My poor husband.

Now, to complement what Shijin is saying, I noticed I could let go of my P (at least so far) and my thyroid medicine is really making the difference for me. So she's right about the components. It's finding the balance. I think I mentioned to you in another thread that maybe you should consider underlying issues. As far as diet goes, maybe you are not getting all that you think you are? I take HCL 3x day, I eat super healthy foods, and yet cannot for the life of me improve my keratosis pilaris (vit A deficiency) and everytime I stop taking fish oil, my nails go to pot. This is despite taking FCLO/butter oil blend and eating ridiculous amounts of fish/oysters/sardines...

Do you have methylation issues? Lyme? Absorption? Additional food/chemical sensitivities?

### HoneyChild — 2013-04-17T14:28:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-progesterone.6797/post-84327>

I assume that progesterone increases metabolic rate by increasing temperature which I think could then lead to a relative iodine deficiency/dehydration. I'm not taking any hormones of any kind but the last couple of cycles I've been waking up in the middle of the night very hot and thirsty around days 19-22 of my cycle which is when progesterone is supposed to peak. Still haven't had my ZRT tests back so I can only speculate on what's happening, I think my samples may have got lost in the post.

> "When one begins eating a paleo diet your nutrient density and food substrate improve so your iodine needs go up as your mitochondria are being asked to do more. So with a paleo diet you get a relative iodine deficiency for the first 3-6 months of the adaptation of the diet.
>
> \[...\]
>
> Why does the classic paleo diet make your LDL rise so often? The answer is simpleâ€¦â€¦dehydration is favored when you eat lots of protein and starchy carbs. A large amount of protein and starchy carbs draws water and even will make your temperature rise. For every degree your temperature increases your basal metabolic rate increase 13%. This increase in temperature, causes water dehydration in your carbon nanotubes. If you add back water that is fluoridated you will not improve. The water must be sping, mineral, or treated with reverse osmosis or you will have severe low carb flu present. ironically this is what the paleosphere calls it. I do not."
> [http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-rx-the-top-ten-emf-faqs/](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-rx-the-top-ten-emf-faqs/)

Progesterone apparently also allows thyroid hormones to function better. As Jack has said, the thyroid is needed for uncoupling proteins to work so that you can release excess calories as heat at night. So I think this is what's happening to me.

> "According to the late clinician John R Lee, M.D., estrogen dominance is behind many cases of midlife hypothyroidism, in which there are inadequate levels of thyroid hormone. When estrogen is not properly counterbalanced with progesterone, Dr. Lee surmised, it can block the action of thyroid hormone, so that even when the thyroid is producing normal levels of the hormone, the hormone is rendered ineffective and the symptoms of hypothyroidism appear. In this case, laboratory tests may show normal thyroid hormone levels in a womanâ€™s system, because the thyroid gland itself is not malfunctioning. This problem is compounded when a woman is prescribed supplemental estrogen, which then leads to an even greater imbalance. Prescribing supplemental thyroid hormone in that case will fail to correct the underlying problem: estrogen dominance. "
> [http://www.drnorthrup.com/womenshealth/healthcenter/topic\_details.php?topic\_id=59](http://www.drnorthrup.com/womenshealth/healthcenter/topic_details.php?topic_id=59)

This is also an interesting article about progesterone and thyroid function: [http://www.health-truth.com/126.php](http://www.health-truth.com/126.php)

But towards the end I really started gaining weight in weird ways and feeling depressed, hating everything (in this last way reverting back to pre-progesterone days).
>
> I have always felt random impulses to move across the country, buy/sell a house/ get a dog, change cars... paint... whatever. I just needed change. For a while, in the good moments of progesterone usage, this calmed down and I was so happy. Then it came back. My poor husband.
>
> Now, to complement what Shijin is saying, I noticed I could let go of my P (at least so far) and my thyroid medicine is really making the difference for me. So she's right about the components. It's finding the balance. I think I mentioned to you in another thread that maybe you should consider underlying issues. As far as diet goes, maybe you are not getting all that you think you are? I take HCL 3x day, I eat super healthy foods, and yet cannot for the life of me improve my keratosis pilaris (vit A deficiency) and everytime I stop taking fish oil, my nails go to pot. This is despite taking FCLO/butter oil blend and eating ridiculous amounts of fish/oysters/sardines...
>

This is what I'm worried about - that I'll have a "honeymoon" period with the Pg. That's what seemed to happen when I first started taking it, but then it's like I have to constantly increase every few weeks/months in order to maintain that good feeling. If I really think about it though, I think I have always had random impulses for most of my adult life - much before I started the Pg, so I guess I can't blame it completely on that. And it's a totally cyclical thing for me too. It comes and goes. I really need to start doing a better job with my journaling so I can pinpoint if it happens at a certain time in my cycle or if there's some other cause.

My thyroid finally seems to be headed in the right direction, but it still has me a bit confused. I was supposed to have a consult with JK last week and am really bummed that it wasn't able to happen. My T3 is in range for the first time since I've been testing it the last several years, but my T4 is still low and TSH high. That part confuses me since I thought T4 converts to T3 . . . if that's the case why would my T3 be ok, but my T4 low?? I just can't figure all this out.

My T3 is in range for the first time since I've been testing it the last several years, **but my T4 is still low and TSH high. That part confuses me since I thought T4 converts to T3 . . . if that's the case why would my T3 be ok, but my T4 low??** I just can't figure all this out.
>
>
>
> I love your attitude Gretchen!
>
>
>
> Thanks for this Chrysanthemama. I'm going to check that book out. I really think I do have a major unbalance. All my symptoms seem to point to both E dominance and low E2. I've always been the oddball

so the first thing I bolded is really important! I've used both spreadsheets and written journals - its what's helped me figure out what's going on, and it can definitely help show your doc what's going on as well...

As for the High TSH, low T4 and T3 being in range - HOW DO YOU FEEL? Do you feel great w/a HIGH TSH? if not push for more meds! I've finally gotten my TSH down to .45, my T4 is still low, but my T3 is freaking out of RANGE! - BUT I FEEL AWESOME! Ranges are just reference points for you to start from - and compare where you're at with HOW YOU FEEL at that given point. Not all of us are going to fit the "Bell curve". Remember N=1. your thyroid results are telling you this. Now go back and look at your thyroid labs - sit quietly and listen to your body - what does it say about your thyroid labs?

going back to your question - about how can T4 be low and T3 be high - well what T4 your making is converting to T3 - but the question is your T3 high b/c the receptor cells are being blocked by rT3? what's that look like? Additionally your TSH is in high gear - trying to get traction - your pituitary is telling your Thyroid - HEY WAKE UP AND MAKE T4!!!!!!

here's a good explanation of how T4 is converted to T3 and some things that can get in the way of conversion
[http://www.naturalendocrinesolutions.com/articles/do-you-have-a-t4-to-t3-conversion-problem/](http://www.naturalendocrinesolutions.com/articles/do-you-have-a-t4-to-t3-conversion-problem/)

And this one does a nice job explaining how to look at the process of how thyroid funcitons in the body
[http://health.howstuffworks.com/human-body/systems/endocrine/understanding-thyroid-metabolism.htm](http://health.howstuffworks.com/human-body/systems/endocrine/understanding-thyroid-metabolism.htm)

Here's the thing - we're all oddballs, why b/c we're swimming against the tide as we try to fix ourselves. we're not willing to settle for feeling crappy or Ok. We want to feel great. We want to feel awesome. WE DON'T WANT TO BE SICK ANY LONGER! WE'VE HAD ENOUGH OF THE CURRENT SYSTEM. WE'RE TAKING CONTROL OF OUR HEALTH AND HOW WE'RE GOING TO LIVE OUR LIVES... this is a good thing. Embrace being the odd duck out. go ahead - Let your freak flag fly!
