# Thread: circadian disruption classified as carcinogen....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2013-05-10T18:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85794>

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/781058?src=wnl\_edit\_jrl&uac=98643BN](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/781058?src=wnl_edit_jrl&uac=98643BN)

### nonchalant — 2013-05-10T19:02:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85796>

Can you quote a paragraph? I don't have a login.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85798>

Have to do it in multiple posts...too long

Abstract
Objectives Animal evidence suggests that circadian disruption may be associated with ovarian cancer, though very little epidemiological work has been done to assess this potential association. We evaluated the association between self-reported nightshift work, a known circadian disruptor, and ovarian cancer in a population-based case-control study.
Methods The study included 1101 women with invasive epithelial ovarian cancer, 389 women with borderline epithelial ovarian tumours and 1832 controls and was conducted in western Washington state. Shift work data were collected as part of inperson interviews.
Results Working the nightshift was associated with an increased risk of invasive (OR=1.24, 95% CI 1.04 to 1.49) and borderline (OR=1.48, 95% CI 1.15 to 1.90) tumours; however, we observed little evidence that risks increased with increasing cumulative duration of nightshift work, and risks were not elevated in the highest duration category (>7 nightshift work-years). Increased risks were restricted to women who were 50 years of age and older and to serous and mucinous histologies of invasive and borderline tumours. There was suggestive evidence of a decreased risk of ovarian cancer among women reporting a preference for activity during evenings rather than mornings.
Conclusions We found evidence suggesting an association between shift work and ovarian cancer. This observation should be followed up in future studies incorporating detailed assessments of diurnal preference (ie, chronotype) in addition to detailed data on shift schedules.
Introduction
It is estimated that over 22 000 new cases of ovarian cancer will be diagnosed in the USA in 2012, with over 15 000 deaths from the disease.\[1\] There are few known factors that influence risk of ovarian cancer, including hormonal contraceptive use, parity and heavier body weight.\[2\] Given the high mortality rate among patients with ovarian cancer, the identification of potentially modifiable risk factors is crucial to disease prevention efforts.
Shift work resulting in circadian disruption has been classified as a probable human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer.\[3\] Though the mechanisms behind the carcinogenicity of shift work are not fully understood, they are thought to be mediated by the indoleamine melatonin, which is produced at night-time and suppressed by ambient light.\[4\] Melatonin is a regulator of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, and the effect of melatonin on reproductive hormones, including oestrogen, has been considered a primary mechanism by which shift work may be associated with breast cancer risk.\[5\]

This mechanism may also extend to ovarian cancer for which there is evidence that endogenous reproductive hormones are involved in the pathogenesis of the disease.\[6\] Evidence suggests that melatonin may be directly involved in ovarian function, including follicular development, ovulation, oocyte maturation and luteal function.\[7\] Extensive animal and cellular experimental data have shown melatonin to have direct oncostatic properties which may be mediated through an effect on reactive oxygen species.\[8\] In fact, in a study of rats, oral administration of melatonin was shown to decrease lipid hydroperoxide levels, increase total levels of antioxidant substances and increase the activity of antioxidant enzymes, including superoxide dismutase, catalase and glutathione-reductase in ovarian tissue.\[9\]
Despite this suggestive experimental evidence, few epidemiological studies have examined the association between shift work and ovarian cancer. Here we describe a large population-based case-control study in which we examined the relation of lifetime nightshift work with risk of epithelial ovarian tumours.
Materials and Methods
Study Population
Female residents of a 13-county area of western Washington state who were diagnosed with a primary invasive or borderline epithelial ovarian tumour from 2002 to 2009 were considered eligible as cases. From 2002 to 2005, women aged 35–74 years were included, while from 2006 to 2009 only women aged 35–69 years were included. The cases were identified through a population-based cancer registry, the Cancer Surveillance System (CSS), which is part of the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results Program of the US National Cancer Institute. The methods for case ascertainment and for case and control recruitment for 2002–2005 have been previously described;\[10\] similar methods were used for 2006–2009.\[11\] After excluding 61 cases because of a potential language barrier, 2025 eligible cases were identified, and 1502 (74.2%) were interviewed. Of the interviewed cases, 1108 had invasive disease and 394 had borderline tumours. Histological type was collected and coded by the CSS using the International Classification of Diseases for Oncology\[12\] morphology codes and grouped according to the guidelines of WHO\[13\] as serous, mucinous, endometrioid, clear cell and other epithelial tumours (comprised largely of unspecified adenocarcinoma and carcinoma). Tumour grade was also collected by the CSS as grades 1 (well differentiated) through 4 (undifferentiated) or unknown. In addition to assessing risk of invasive and borderline tumours separately, we created the following additional analytic groupings of invasive cancers, based on a recent conceptualisation of ovarian cancer pathogenesis:\[14 15\] high-grade serous (HGSC; comprised of serous morphology, grades 2–4 or unknown, as well as grade 3 or higher tumours that were morphologically classified as endometrioid; n=840); low-grade serous (LGSC; grade 1 serous; n=23); mucinous (MC; n=32); endometrioid (EC; morphologically endometrioid and grade 1, 2 or unknown; n=129); and clear cell tumours (CCC; n=84). Based on molecular similarities observed between (1) borderline serous tumours and invasive LGSC and (2) borderline and invasive mucinous tumours, we further combined invasive and borderline tumours of these subtypes. Other borderline tumours (n=23) were excluded from the subtype analyses.
Controls were selected by random-digit dialling (RDD) using stratified sampling in 5-year age categories, 1-year calendar intervals and two county strata. From 2002 to 2005, a 2:1 ratio of controls to women with invasive ovarian cancer was selected using the Waksberg–Mitofsky RDD method.\[16\] From 2006 to 2009, a 1:1 ratio of controls to women with invasive disease was used; list-assisted RDD\[17\] was employed during 2006–2007 and Waksberg–Mitofsky methods during 2008–2009. In total, for 19 092 (78.2%) of the 24 400 telephone numbers belonging to residences, we determined whether an eligible woman (ie, an age and county eligible woman able to communicate in English and, if so, with at least one ovary and no prior history of ovarian cancer) resided there. Of the 2351 eligible women identified, 1849 were interviewed (78.6%). The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, and all women provided signed informed consent before participating.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85799>

Data Collection
Inperson interviews pertained to the period of time before diagnosis (for cases) or before an assigned comparable reference date (for controls), and covered the following: demographic and lifestyle characteristics; employment history; medical history; and detailed reproductive history, including menstrual, pregnancy, and use of contraceptive and menopausal hormone preparations.
In addition to start and end dates and average hours per week for each job held at least a continuous 4 months starting at age 25 until the reference date, participants were asked to report how many of their work days over the duration of the job included the hours between midnight and 04:00 (nightshift), to which they could respond: never worked those hours; less than half the days; at least half the days; or every work day. As a measure of chronotype (preference for being active in the morning or the evening), which may be associated with the ability to adapt to nightshift work, participants were asked to report if they were morning persons, evening persons or other (assessed as of 5 years before the reference date).
Shift Work Variables
For the primary analysis, two shift work variables were created: ever/never worked any nightshift and cumulative nightshift work-years (from age 25 until the reference date). For each participant with j jobs, cumulative nightshift work-years (CN) was calculated as follows:

where Dj is the total number of months engaged in a particular job, Hj is the hours worked per week for that job and Fj is the fraction of time spent doing nightshift work for that job. For each job, Fj was assigned as 0 if the subject reported that she never worked nightshifts, 0.25 if she reported that less than half of the days involved nightshifts, 0.5 if she reported that at least half of the days involved nightshifts or 1.0 if she reported that every work day involved nightshifts. Work-years were calculated by dividing the total number of hours engaged in nightshift work for a particular job by 2080, which is the total number of hours worked in a year assuming an average of 40 h of work per week.
We also examined ever/never worked in a job with less than half of work days doing the nightshift and ever/never worked a job with all nightshifts. These categorisations were not mutually exclusive, that is, a participant reporting working one or more jobs with less than half of works days doing the nightshift and one or more jobs with all nightshifts would be in the 'ever' exposed category in both analyses.
Statistical Analysis
Unconditional logistic regression was used to calculate ORs and corresponding 95% CI. All analyses were adjusted for the frequency-matching variables of age at reference (5-year intervals), county of residence (dichotomised as the three urban or 10 rural/suburban counties in the study) and calendar year of diagnosis/reference date (continuous). We also adjusted for factors known to be associated with ovarian cancer: duration of hormonal contraceptive use (never, <6, 6–59, 60–119 or ≥120 months), number of full term pregnancies (0, 1, 2 or ≥3) and body mass index (BMI) at age 30 (<18.5, 18.5–24.9, 25–29.9 or ≥30). Adjustment for additional factors including race/ethnicity, history of hormone replacement therapy, smoking status, alcohol use and education had a minimal impact (ie, < 10% change) on point estimates of interest, so these variables were not included in the final analyses. Cumulative nightshift work-years were categorised according to approximate quartiles among controls who were ever engaged in nightshift work. Median number of work-years was also compared between cases and controls using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test given the highly skewed distribution of the variable.
Additional separate analyses were conducted to examine the association of shift work with risk of ovarian cancer among women diagnosed before and after age 50 years and to examine the association of shift work with tumour subtypes (as described above). We also evaluated whether chronotype (morning or evening preference) modified the association between shift work and ovarian cancer. Participants who did not indicate a morning or evening preference were excluded from this analysis. Effect modification was formally evaluated using likelihood ratio tests comparing models with and without chronotype and nightshift work cross-products terms. All analyses were carried out using the STATA V.11 statistical package (Statacorp LP, College Station, Texas, USA).

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:40:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85800>

Results
After excluding 29 subjects with missing covariate data on BMI, number of births or duration of hormonal contraception, a total of 1101 invasive cancer cases, 389 borderline tumour cases and 1832 controls were included in our analyses. The distribution of various characteristics of cases and controls is provided in Table 1. Compared with controls, a lesser proportion of invasive cases used hormonal contraceptives, and invasive and borderline cases tended to have had fewer children than controls. As expected, women with borderline tumours tended to be younger than women with invasive disease.
Among the 27% of invasive cases that reported ever working a night job (Table 2), the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 3.5, and among the 32% of borderline cases, the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 3.2. Among the 23% of controls working night jobs, the median number of work-years engaged in the nightshift was 2.7; these differences between the case groups and controls were not statistically significant (p>0.5). Among controls and invasive tumour cases, the majority of jobs that were reported as involving nightshift work were in healthcare (27% and 28%, respectively), mostly nursing, followed by food preparation and service (eg, cooks, waitresses, bartenders) (18% and 16%) and office and administrative support (eg, telephone operators, customer service representatives, dispatchers) (17% and 15%). Among borderline cases, the majority of nightshift jobs were in food preparation and service (24%), followed by healthcare (20%) and office and administrative support (13%).
Ever working the nightshift was associated with a 1.24-fold increased risk of invasive epithelial ovarian cancer (95% CI 1.04 to 1.49) and a 1.48-fold increased risk of borderline epithelial ovarian tumours (95% CI 1.15 to 1.90). When examining cumulative nightshift work-years, there were no indications of a trend with either invasive or borderline tumours; significant increases in risk were restricted to the second highest cumulative category (>3–7 work-years).
In additional analyses, risks of invasive and borderline tumours among women who reported ever working a job with less than half of the work days as nights (ORinvasive=1.28, 95% CI 1.03 to 1.59; ORborderline=1.32, 95% CI 0.98 to 1.78) and who reported working a job with all nights (ORinvasive=1.16, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.55; ORborderline=1.29, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.92) were not materially different from those observed in the main analyses evaluating any history of ever working the nightshift (Table 2). A greater percentage of evening type individuals had ever engaged in nightshift work than had morning types (27% vs 20% of controls; χ2, p=0.002); while the risks of invasive and borderline ovarian tumours for those ever working the nightshift among self-classified morning type individuals (ORinvasive=1.29, 95% CI 1.00 to 1.67; ORborderline=1.57, 95% CI 1.08 to 2.27) was slightly greater than those ever working the nightshift among self-classified evening type individuals (ORinvasive=1.14, 95% CI 0.85 to 1.53; ORborderline=1.43, 95% CI 0.97 to 2.09), the differential effects were not statistically significant (pinvasive=0.47; pborderline=0.54).

When stratifying by reference age, ever working the nightshift was significantly associated with increased risks of both invasive and borderline tumours among women who were 50 years of age or older (Table 3), but risks were not significantly elevated in women under 50 years of age. These apparent differential affects by age were not statistically significant (pinvasive=0.32; pborderline=0.65). Table 4 presents the results of the subtype-specific analyses of invasive and borderline disease. Increased risks of HGSC and LGSC (the latter including borderline and invasive disease) were associated with ever working the nightshift (ORs=1.29, 95% CI 1.06 to 1.57, and 1.51, 95% CI 1.12 to 2.05, respectively). Risk of MC (including invasive and borderline disease) was also positively associated with ever working the nightshift (OR=1.55, 95% CI 1.10 to 2.17). In contrast, overall risks of both EC and CCC were, if anything, slightly reduced in association with ever working the nightshift (OR=0.91 for each of these subtypes). To assess whether subtype-specific differences were simply reflecting an age effect or vice versa, we conducted age-specific analyses within histological subgroups. No clear differences in risk by age within the EC, CCC, MC and LGSC subgroups were observed, while risks among the most common subtype, HGSC, were primarily increased among women aged 50 or more years (data not shown)
Discussion
In our large population-based case-control study, we found evidence suggesting an association between ever working the nightshift and increased risk of epithelial ovarian tumours. The increased risks that we observed are consistent in magnitude with those observed for breast cancer in previous studies.\[18\] However, we did not observe a trend with increasing cumulative duration of nightshift work nor did we observe an increased risk in the highest category of cumulative duration. There was suggestive evidence that increased risks were restricted to women 50 years of age and older. In tumour subtype analyses, elevated risks associated with nightshift work were observed for HGSC, LGSC and MC subtypes (the last two including both invasive and borderline tumours), but not with EC or CCC. While risks associated with most reproductive factors have been consistent across all histological types,\[19\] associations with certain factors such as smoking, endometriosis and BMI have been observed to vary by histology.\[19–21\]
Chronotype has been considered by others as a measure of adaptability to shift work schedules, with evening type individuals reporting better tolerance to nightshift work (eg, better work performance and higher job satisfaction) than morning type individuals.\[22\] In the current study, a greater proportion of evening type individuals among our controls had a history of nightshift work, but there was only suggestive evidence that ovarian cancer was elevated to a lesser extent among such individuals than among morning type persons.
In a recent prospective analysis of 718 ovarian cancer cases among 181 548 participants in the Nurse's Health Study, there was no evidence of an association between rotating shift work and ovarian cancer risk.\[23\] Rotating shift work was defined as working at least three nights per month in addition to day or evening shifts and has been previously associated with breast, endometrial and colorectal cancers in the cohort.\[24–26\] The study did not collect data on permanent/fixed nightshift work. Though our study did not specifically ascertain data on rotating shift work, it is reasonable to assume a rotating shift work schedule for those reporting working jobs with less than half of the work days involving the nightshift. We found evidence of increased risks of invasive ovarian cancer in association with nightshift work even when restricting analyses to this group. It is difficult to directly compare the results of our study with those of the Nurse's Health Study given the differences in shift work data that were collected.

### cantweight — 2013-05-10T19:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-disruption-classified-as-carcinogen.7014/post-85801>

Animal and laboratory data suggest that an increased risk of ovarian cancer associated with circadian disruption may be plausible. In turkey breeder hens with ovarian adenocarcinomas, longer day periods (ie, longer exposure to light) promoted growth of ovarian tumours, while shorter day periods were found to inhibit growth.\[27\] Direct administration of melatonin was also found to inhibit tumour growth. As has been hypothesised for breast cancer, melatonin may impact ovarian cancer risk through an effect on endogenous levels of reproductive hormones, particularly oestrogen. Though evidence suggests that oestrogens may be especially important in the aetiology of EC and CCC,\[6\] we did not observe associations between nightshift work and risk of these subtypes. Beyond its effects on reproductive hormones, melatonin has been shown to have direct oncostatic properties. In addition to directly scavenging reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, melatonin stimulates other antioxidants and enzymes that metabolise reactive species.\[7\] In a study of female rats, ethanol was found to increase lipid hydroperoxide levels in ovarian tissue; with concurrent melatonin administration, lipid hydroperoxide levels were significantly decreased, and the antioxidant activities of superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase and glutathione reductase were all increased.\[28\]
The large size of our study and comprehensive data on historic nightshift schedules, as well as important potential confounders, are strengths. Though participation rates were relatively high for both cases and controls, participation bias may have influenced study results if those women who chose not to participate in our study differed from study subjects in factors related to nightshift work. Although data on nightshift work were retrospectively collected through self-report, the potential association of shift work with ovarian cancer was not a primary hypothesis of the study that was expressed to participants, so recall bias is unlikely. Further, while study participants may have been aware of light-at-night as a hypothesised risk factor for other cancers, little or no attention had been paid to ovarian cancer during the years in which our study was conducted. The prevalence of shift work in our study population is higher than what has been reported for the general US working population by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) (17.7% among US salaried workers).\[29\] However, the higher prevalence is consistent with the types of jobs that study subjects reported working. For example, among controls, the most commonly worked jobs involving the nightshift were in the healthcare industry, and according to the BLS, over 24% of salaried healthcare workers in the US were engaged in shift work in 2004. Food preparation and service was the next most common class of job involving nightshift work that controls reported. According to BLS data, over 49% of salaried US food preparation and service workers were engaged in shift work in 2004.
We did not observe a significant dose–response relationship with cumulative nightshift work-years, which may be attributable to exposure misclassification. For instance, the fraction of time spent working the nightshift for a particular job could, in actuality, be any value between 0 and 1, but for this study, we were only able to assign one of four values to this fraction. Also, information on intensity of nightshift schedules was not ascertained as part of this study, meaning that a participant working a full-time job with two nights per week over a period of 10 years would be treated the same in our analyses as a participant working the nightshift for the first 2 years of a full-time job that lasted for 10 years. In addition, the exposure variables, while relatively detailed, may not have captured the aspect of exposure most linked with risk. For example, with rotating shift work schedules, the rate of rotation and direction of rotation may be particularly important,\[30\] but that information was not collected as part of this study. We did, however, have data on chronotype, which may be a critical modifier of the association between nightshift work and cancer but mostly has not been evaluated in studies of nightshift work and cancer risk. However, this variable was based on a response to a single question, and there are much more comprehensive assessment tools available, such as the Circadian Type Inventory (18-item questionnaire), which assign scores to classify individuals on a morningness–eveningness scale.\[31\] Chance must also be considered as an explanation of our findings.

We found suggestive evidence that nightshift work is associated with increased risks of both invasive and borderline ovarian tumours, particularly of serous and mucinous subtypes and among women who are 50 years of age and older. However, our results contrast with those of the single prior study of ovarian cancer risk that evaluated rotating shift work. Further research is needed, and future studies should incorporate assessments of chronotype to further explore whether evening-type persons are at a decreased risk of developing ovarian cancer as a result of better adaptability to shift work schedules. These studies should also evaluate the impact of genetic variation in circadian genes in conjunction with chronotype on susceptibility to the potential carcinogenic effects of nightshift work. Prospectively designed studies are likely to provide the best option for collecting the detailed high-quality shift work data that are needed. However, for rare diseases such as ovarian cancer, individual cohorts may lack power to detect significant associations, particularly with respect to stratification on chronotype, genetic variation or tumour morphology

AC when it works produces lots of condensate (water).

Martin suffers from exposure to black mold.

It would be nice to discuss ways to avoid this.

### Martin — 2013-05-11T23:59:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-specifics-on-buying-an-ac.7021/post-85858>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=85851)
>
> I am also interested in this EMF type of question.
> But I also have another AC/heat related question.
>
> AC when it works produces lots of condensate (water).

> All is well when all is working as it should.

> Martin suffers from exposure to black mold.
>
> It would be nice to discuss ways to avoid this.
>
>
> ....

We opted not to have humidifier on our system and I'm glad we did. It was a money issue at the time.

I wonder how the humidifying systems _really_ work after having all these problems with new equipment.

Some easy things are visual inspections of the unit downwind of the "condensing coil", the gizmo located in the ductwork system where the copper and black foam rubber covered refrigerant tubes go into the side of the metal cabinet. There is an inspection cover with screws and it's easy to remove and shine a light into and inspect.

Before I discovered the leaking, I was just checking the drain that drips water from the AC to the outside, and while it was still flowing, I figured it was all right. Turns ou the drip pan was warped and the water was going out two ways - dripping into my ducts, and out the drain. Fooled!

I noticed my problem when I finally got around to crawling under the house and saw/heard it was dripping water onto the ground below it. There was the catch pan, the one that failed. Also, we chose fiberglass duct board material instead of metal to deaden sound and provide insulation, and, a little cheaper. Turns out duct board is a fabo mold growing medium when soaked with water. Replaced all ducting with metal, louder, but safer.

Inspection schedule is key. Just because it's _new_ don't mean it ain't going bonkers...

This video is an excellent demonstration of Lord Kelvin's water dropper experiment, where just dropping water from one bottle into two different interconnected vessels creates bursts of thousands of volts of electricity. Sorry Glen, I just had to watch it. ;-)

I don't have Dr. Pollack's book, but I would have to guess that the effect is a quantum one. The cross-wiring is creating a paradox of sorts for the poor little water drops. They drop into container A, but part of the experience is transferred to container B instead. They must be really confused until the difference is resolved. And I would guess that the "spreading effect" on the water stream is similar to the water bridges across the tops of beakers, where the energy is trying to flow from one vessel to the other.

### Josh — 2014-01-11T04:35:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-book-now-available-by-professor-gerald-pollack.7022/post-105341>

Now I am reading Pollack's book and everything is popping for me. The behavior of water in confined spaces over a charged surface creating nucleation and a zone of very different behaving stuff with a zone around it with radically different chemistry and behavior as well. The pictures really help and then I can scale the ideas back out into the biology and universe. I have a friend who I have called a "water genius". He could not read until he was 18 because of dyslexia. He has a great story of how the community college English professor told him to fail a few times so she could teach him to read and write well. He can now speed read and writes mostly with Dragon Speech. He built a plane that flew, but broke up with him in it 25 feet off the ground when he was 9. One day he was driving across East Texas and he got caught in a tornado. He found himself holding onto a tree inside the tornado and he said the behavior of the water inside the tornado awed him to the point that he decided to study water. Reading Pollack's book, I am getting an idea of what the water may have looked like inside the tornado and inside our nano tubules of the mitochondria...For me this is a breakthrough in picturing what might be going on. The diagrams are helping me turn on my thinking in pictures brain to mock things up and test them. There are also pictures of many of the people we discuss such as Ling and Mae Wan Ho which makes them more real for me. I know I can use Google images, and the book is more personal. My water friend says his water world friends have been happy that the book was published because now they have something that helps them talk about their work. Better late than never....

### cmegan — 2013-05-12T18:41:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-supplementation.7033/post-85899>

Hi!

Does anyone know the differences, benefits, etc. of supplementing with Vit D on a daily basis (5,000 u) as opposed to the once-per-week mega doses (50,000 u)?

It's really weird, but when I dose with the daily dose I seem to get side effects like headaches and extreme thirst. When I dosed before on the weekly mega dose I didn't get any side effects. I am wondering if I am just imagining these weird side effects on the daily dose. I am taking LEF formula.

Thank you!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-12T20:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-supplementation.7033/post-85902>

Answer your own question.........with common sense. The sun makes Vitamin D from cholesterol in our skin with water........using the photoelectric effect.......do you think we are designed to make it once per week? We are designed to make it daily........If you are getting headaches and extreme thirst in an indoor existence in a blue lit world filled with nnEMF. I seriously doubt it is Vitamin D that is the problem. I believe one cannot make Vitamin D properly when these conditions exist because of a lack of redox.........it tells me you have an extreme light pollution issue and you might have a serious Vitamin A problem due to melanopsin dysfunction.

Vitamin A issues are rare......but vitamin A in excess can cause pseudotumor cerebri and brain swelling. when the problem is not as severe smaller constellation of symptoms that mimic those in pseudotumor will exist. This is a symptom seen in phase-shifting due to circadian mismatches. Vitamin A is 100% linked to the photoperiodicity in your life......and is tightly regulated in the brain provided the light cycle is being adhered too.......if it is not.......your symptoms are what we see.........an ASI and salivary melatonin tell you just how bad you are. And if you are........off vitamin A links to the RXR receptor and this wires to how we tell of DON'T tell the quantum time in our organ clocks. When this is off it directly ties to PER1 and PER 2 clock genes (Rev erb A and B)........and molecular chaos ensues.........as headaches go on......psych diseases show up next..........and others diseases to follow.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-12T20:21:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-supplementation.7033/post-85903>

Here is a an excerpt from a comment I made from a blog called finding your inner masterpiece:To understand the leptin story you need to really read EMF 2 blog post........about how leptin really works in the hypothalamus. The connections will be made for you. PPARG regulates fatty acid storage and glucose metabolism but leptin is designed to interact with it when cellular signaling is working well to make fat.......when we are energy inefficient leptin and PPAR gamma no longer work in allosteric balance to do what evolution designed them to do. The genes activated by PPARG stimulate lipid uptake and adipogenesis by fat cells normally. So high leptin levels make you more fat but storing excess energy. The key thing is you can tap the energy when you are LR. PPAR G is all about filling the dump truck not emptying it. Naturally lean folks never really fill it because their semi conductors use the energy efficiently......so never activate PPAR G. PPAR G needs Retinoic X receptors to heterodimerize with it to work. So this brings in the Vitamin A story to evolution........those who are LR have a real problem with Vitamin recycling that directly impacts the RXR function. Binding of agonist ligands to RXR results in dissociation of corepressor and recruitment of coactivator protein, which, in turn, promotes transcription of the downstream target gene into mRNA and eventually protein. This is broken in the LR. Why? Vitamin A is directly tied to circadian timing and the photoperiod. This means Vitamin A levels should swing wildly in seasonal change and it does. Excessive light pollution from modern technology causes allosteric and homeostatic issues in the Vitamin A cycle in the brain. This directly affect gene expression by alteration in the molecular organ clocks body wide, but the brain really takes a hit because the hypothalamus loses control of its ability to balance hormones because it can no longer tell quantum time using the photoperiod. This answer requires a lot of knot tying in the EMF series and the QB series........but this is the answer you asked for even it does hurt your head. Paying strict attention to light cycles, then meal timing trumps food choices in how evolution really works........

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-12T20:26:29+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-14T17:48:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-all-you-to-act-on-this.7049/post-86023>

Thomas E. Wheeler, a former lobbyist for CTIA who opposed meaningful RF safety limits in the 1996 Telecommunications Act, has been nominated to be Chairman of the FCC.

Please help us to educate the US Senate Committee on Commerce, Science & Transportation about the serious conflicts of interest that Mr. Wheeler has that mean he is unfit to be Chairman of the FCC.

Below you will find a sample letter, please personalize it by inserting a statement about your own experience between paragraph 4 and 5 or delete reminder placeholder, and add your name and address at the top and your name at the bottom. A brief first-hand experience illustrating the need for biologically-based RF safety limits and maintaining the landline phone system would add power to your letter.

Please send your letter AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. Mr. Wheeler's confirmation hearings begin soon.

Below is a list of email addresses to send your letter to. Please copy and paste it into the TO line of your email.

If you have not already done so, please promote the petition on twitter, facebook, websites, and in newsletters, links below.

Thank you for your continued support,

The EMRadiation Policy Institute

Here's the petition link:
[http://www.change.org/petitions/cha...-hire-epa-to-set-rf-radiation-safety-limits-4](http://www.change.org/petitions/chairman-rockefeller-senate-committee-on-commerce-science-transportation-hold-hearings-to-fire-fcc-and-hire-epa-to-set-rf-radiation-safety-limits-4)
On Facebook at: [https://www.facebook.com/pages/EMR-Policy-Institute/188070864579167](https://www.facebook.com/pages/EMR-Policy-Institute/188070864579167)
On Twitter at: [https://twitter.com/@emrpolicy](https://twitter.com/@emrpolicy)

Please help us to educate the US Senate Committee on Commerce, Science & Transportation about the serious conflicts of interest that Mr. Wheeler has that mean he is unfit to be Chairman of the FCC.

Below you will find a sample letter, please personalize it by inserting a statement about your own experience between paragraph 4 and 5 or delete reminder placeholder, and add your name and address at the top and your name at the bottom. A brief first-hand experience illustrating the need for biologically-based RF safety limits and maintaining the landline phone system would add power to your letter.

Please send your letter AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. Mr. Wheeler's confirmation hearings begin soon.

Below is a list of email addresses to send your letter to. Please copy and paste it into the TO line of your email.

If you have not already done so, please promote the petition on twitter, facebook, websites, and in newsletters, links below.

Thank you for your continued support,

The EMRadiation Policy Institute

Here's the petition link:
[http://www.change.org/petitions/cha...-hire-epa-to-set-rf-radiation-safety-limits-4](http://www.change.org/petitions/chairman-rockefeller-senate-committee-on-commerce-science-transportation-hold-hearings-to-fire-fcc-and-hire-epa-to-set-rf-radiation-safety-limits-4)
On Facebook at: [https://www.facebook.com/pages/EMR-Policy-Institute/188070864579167](https://www.facebook.com/pages/EMR-Policy-Institute/188070864579167)
On Twitter at: [https://twitter.com/@emrpolicy](https://twitter.com/@emrpolicy)

# Thread: Committing to a one month CT experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 124
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2013-05-15T04:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86053>

In the Women's Fat Loss webinar Dr. K. says something like "CT is your exercise" so here goes. I'm committing to a one-month CT experiment starting this weekend. My plan is to eat strictly epi-paleo and CT a minimum of 5 times a week in waist-deep water. I'll take regular measurements so I have some objective criteria rather than just my jeans fitting better. My goal is to reduce abdominal fat and tone up.

Although I really suck about posting regularly on the Forum, I got the idea to commit publicly from Dr. K's book where where he talks about using social media to hold each other accountable. By posting here, I'm making myself accountable to all of you.

### Lava — 2013-05-15T05:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86055>

OK awesome!!! I am doing it with you...

### curves64 — 2013-05-15T05:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86056>

AWESOME LAVA!!!!! I'm so excited to have your company!!!

### colt — 2013-05-15T05:21:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86057>

Guys not that you need to hear more stories but my starting weight was 210. 3 months and I weigh 178 lbs. What's crazy is my skin tags have dried up and fallen off, my strength levels have not changed. (if anything they have gone up) and my energy levels are through the roof. In case you dont know.... when you lose that kind of weight, its damn near impossible to get stronger or not lose strength. Thats incredible. I CT 3-5 times per week. Started in tub and worked up to the lake (46 degrees). My blood pressure went down so much i wont post it cause you wont believe it.
Trigger

### Lava — 2013-05-15T05:35:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86058>

Curves..lets rock this CT...I hope to get in the ocean most days but if not, I have shut my jacuzzi off and will sit in it....

### caroline — 2013-05-15T07:12:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86066>

### colt — 2013-05-15T12:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86073>

What's crazy is I take pride in trying to do everything right (as far as I know anyway) and it just worked perfectly. Follow the Leptin reset as close to perfect as possible. You'll be amazed.

### cantweight — 2013-05-15T13:39:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86074>

@curves...I'm in....accountability is the swift kick in the ass I was needing

@lava....sounds like you have molluscum contagiosa....its a wart virus often people get it from public swimming areas. Try putting apple cider vinegar on it and spot CT also tea tree oil might be worth a shot....

@trigger....that is awesome. My first shot with leptin and CT was like that. After falling off the wagon 6 times too many I cant seem to get those initial results anymore....so dont stop!!!

@audrey...going into fall was tumultuous for my whole family. Good levels of things dropped, sleep disruptions occurred, eating patterns worsened do to the sleep problems and lack of energy to "do the right things" hang in there, pay close attention to what your body needs to readjust, dont let it get away from you

### SeaHorse — 2013-05-15T14:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86075>

Hey you guys that sounds like a fantastic plan...I'll be cheering you on from my pond (which is getting kind of warm unfortunately).

And just to reinforce what cantweight wrote...seasonal transitions really were difficult for me too. I felt great at the beginning of Sept...and by the end I was cold, tired and hungry. It didn't last and once I committed to the CT things came around and I had a super Nov. Dec. I stopped all the carbs as soon as I could. If you are very sensitive to the light...it's going to happen. That's when your vit D can plummet so quickly...Jk mentioned it happens to him every year too.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T14:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86079>

Are you committed to change yet? Anyone can dabble in it, but once you've made that commitment, it's very hard to stop you. A committed person never gives up, despite all the obstacles they face, they keep walking towards their destiny not allowing even the slightest discomfort to distract them. That's the difference between interest and commitment. When you're interested in doing something, you do it only when circumstance permits, but when you're committed to something, you accept no excuses, only results. Commitment is the reason why some people succeed and others wonder if they could ever. Personal life commitments are centered around three F's. Be Fixed. Be Focused. Be Faithful. Charisma may get you in the front door, but it's the commitment that closes the back door! The first person we have to commit to, is ourselves. A committed mind never falters under a load. Commitment is the reason you might be living your life a little different in order to carry out your vision. People say "how do you find the time?" Time is always there; there was 24 hours in the day for DaVinci just like there is for us all. You don't find the time, you find the commitment to get things done. Today, begin.

### nonchalant — 2013-05-15T16:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86086>

> [Trigger said:](/goto/post?id=86057)
>
> What's crazy is my skin tags have dried up and fallen off, my strength levels have not changed. (if anything they have gone up) and my energy levels are through the roof. In case you dont know.... when you lose that kind of weight, its damn near impossible to get stronger or not lose strength. Thats incredible. I CT 3-5 times per week.
> Trigger

Impossible? Nah. I think everyone who has tried CT for a while has noticed significant strength increases and improved muscle tone. Losing weight while doing CT also requires a reduction in carbs, so the body can take advantage of the ancient pathway. This 30 day experiment is a good idea.

So CT is what caused my skin tags to leave? When one is doing so many things, it is hard to figure out what did the trick. My blood pressure went down significantly when I started paleo, years ago, and allowed me to ditch the meds, but it was still on the borderline of being elevated. Now it is comfortably in the good zone, thanks to CT.

### nonchalant — 2013-05-15T16:09:53+0300

So - I have been wondering what to do about Vit D .....mine was always very low and couldn't bring it up. Once I started supplementing with it last fall - it came up to 149 which is quite high but Jack was happy with that and my next test was 147 and then all the beach time ... so now I just take it every once in awhile. I guess it may have dropped and I should start using it regularly again ....hmmmm. One other thing ..... this summer I have been the most incredible dark brown - I have never ever looked like this before ..... high Vit D???

### freesia — 2013-05-16T01:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86146>

Hi Caroline, I went really brown this past summer too.
I do have a decent Vit D level, so that might be helping ease the seasonal change like others have suggested.

Last Autumn, the first 2 months from when I started CT were difficult. I was feeling so good with Leptin reset though, I think that really helped offset the worst of any CT hardship. The **big** problem for me was with a low body temperature I got very cold, and was also very slow to warm up.
As you know I continued with ice baths all summer. _Despite this_ I still found coming back into Autumn the past month a bit challenging with the cooling ambient air temp. Is that's whats bothering you? Or is a low body temperature a contributing factor too?
Anyway, the past fortnight I felt a bit cool for comfort and I needed an extra layer of clothing....until I got my CT water colder! Colder CT water = more ready heat in response. Last week the tap water got down to mid 50's which means I can get my icebath down to low 40's relatively easily. So this week I'm back in a sun dress and bare feet! I'm registering cold on my skin, under foot especially, but not feeling cold.
...Usually in mid May I'd be shopping up big for merino layers, and hunting out wool gloves, and possum fur inserts for my slippers! My thinking is that since my tap water went down 6 F over the past two weeks, my icebath temp should too. My positive experience doing so reinforces that to me.

I don't know if that's helpful or not to your situation! Those who struggle more to get their internal heater fired up probably have more a complex hormonal profile. It's not nice to feel cold all the time and I feel for you. I felt cold like that most of the time from May to October every year, for decades.

I wonder, for someone who isn't winter adapting quickly - i.e. within the suggested 2 weeks suggested in the CT protocol guidelines - and whose heater _isn't_ firing up consistently, whether more frequent and shorter dips at colder temps might be more effective to get things moving in the right direction? I remember some discussion around this a year ago but for most of us getting water cold enough more than once a day in Autumn, wasn't practical. I only manage it because I have ice delivered...but I'm still early enough in my health recovery to justify those kind of "extreme" measures. Hey I still spend less on ice in a week than some people spend on coffee at Starbucks in a day.
Anyway, I hope you'll figure out what's going to keep you feeling more comfortable. Feeling cold is miserable, so it's worth figuring out.

### caroline — 2013-05-16T02:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86150>

Thanks Ann for all of that - I think it is all of the above! I shouldn't be this cold when it is 70-72 degree. The hunger is driving me crazy and I am gaining weight - very, very unhappy about that. I had no trouble controlling my weight at all the last 6 months - it actually went down but now I am up 3 kilos.

The funny thing is - I am very warm at night. I start off under a duvet but before long my pj's are off and am kicking off everything. Then I get cool and pull the sheet over me and then I get hot again and sweaty ..... and there is no reason for the sweaty - trust me on that one!

I hate the thought of doing C/T where as I enjoyed it before - well - sort of!
My tap water comes out at 75 degrees - haven't checked lately.
I feel like a schlump-a-dink right now!! Gotta refocus and fast! I was feeling so terrific before - makes me want to move to Queensland! I really thrived in the hot weather - never really noticed that before. Could this mean I am meant to live in Darwin???

### caroline — 2013-05-17T00:54:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86262>

Okay - this seasonal thing is very weird - I am up another kilo this morning for no reason - haven't had anything unusual - so now I am up 4 K. It is all in my midsection and I hate it so much - was feeling so terrific before- what the heck happened???

Curves - I understand what you are asking about the amount of food .... I definitely feel the same way.
There is for sure something else that we aren't getting. I feel that my body wants to put on a layer of fat because winter is coming - it is like I have no control whatsoever.
Thank goodness I am not craving anything - I wouldn't thank you for anything sweet or carbs of any kind. I am toying with doing a short round of Hcg. I have been thinking of what I would load with and can't come up with anything other than what I normally eat. This - to me - all means that I have truly adapted epi-paleo and love it - but the seasonal thing is taking control and I can't fight it. I don't like this out of control thing one bit!

I am thinking that we have to alter what we are eating when seasons change. I have been eating primarily fat and some protein but I think it is the fat that is kicking my but! I am also drinking a lot more coffee and tea because I m cold - so as of today - I am stopping that.

Any other ideas anyone?

> I think it means you're vulnerable at the change of seasons! There must be a good clue in that (lol but don't ask me what!) Interesting you're so hot at night despite cooler temps..more clues there I feel.
> Mmmm, love Queensland. I'll never forget visiting in mid-winter and the days were 25 C! I'm not sure if thats typical actually, but it made quite an impression at the time.
> I'd think I'd wilt in Darwin LOL.

Do you feel better in the hot weather? I know you love C/T and it has helped you so much.
I think the hot/sweaty at night is a clue about something! Do you feel as good now - that your furnace has turned back on? or do you feel better in summer?

I seem to have the impression that JK is liking his new location better and enjoys the heat - even tho he loves C/T

so many clues - not enough brain power!

Could it just be that our Vit D takes a big nose dive?

### freesia — 2013-05-17T02:09:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86271>

Hi Caroline, I tolerated last summer's heat easily for once so I think thats the result of "thermoadaptive" improvement...brought about by regular CTing.
So, although I felt good this past summer, (this has been a revelation to me) I dont actually feel _best_ then!
It turns out I feel best in shorter light cycles, especially if I maximize the benefit of that using cold. I think that's a pretty strong indicator of where I'm at with my health. It could be that, among other things, cold stimulates, and helps regulate certain neuropeptides and antioxidant enzymes that seem universally low in people with my illness. I guess my need for those things is higher than my need for sunshine, at least at this point? As far as the temperature goes I do feel as comfortable in Winter now as I did this past Summer...but I did hit that bump a few weeks ago.
So, long story short (!), though summer has obvious benefits (light, sunshine! Vit D), but whatever it is I'm able to harvest during Winter now seems to be even _more_ beneficial. Hope that makes sense. I'm guessing this won't be the case for everyone. I hear of people with ME/CFS doing really well sometimes, when they relocate somewhere warmer or drier.
So many variables huh!

### caroline — 2013-05-17T02:19:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86274>

Yes - so many variables .... you seem to really have a handle on how this all comes together for you - great job - well done!

I need to really figure this out - I don't want to go thru this every fall .. but if I think about it - I probably have always crashed every fall - just thought it was normal. Put on more sweaters and climb under the closest duvet and drink heaps of hot chocolate! But it must bottom out - have to think more about this......I think by Christmas I would feel very invigorated by the cold \[Canada\] I surely wouldn't have spent the whole winter under a down comforter! Too many snowmen to make and snowball fights and hockey arenas etc. etc.

### Inger — 2013-05-17T07:41:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86296>

I loved this winter too freesia! I loved the darkness.. the silence.. the cold dark and boring. It is exactly what I need. I thought it was so cool.. totally magic. Something I had not let happend before ever in my life.

I love to explore new things now. All this Kruse style living has made me quit about any dogmas I have/had, or it has made it way easier to let them go! Can I say how much that is worth.. no. There are no word for that.. so much is it.

True freedom... and that makes me feel like flying.. and sink in to the earth at the same time..lol so weird! Must be the grounding effect and me realizing how much I love the earth and how good it makes me feel

### cantweight — 2013-05-17T14:30:35+0300

### Inger — 2013-05-17T20:33:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86325>

Audrey.. here is what I would do if I had your issues...

Stand as much as I could in the ocean.. minimum 30 min. / day. But you can do way more, take a book with you or something  Who cares if the water is not close to zero degree?  Put a icepack under your neck when going to sleep, I need to start doing that too it is way too warm now at night! Man I miss the sub zero temps in my bedroom in winter...

Tighten my diet = no dairy, and as close to 100% raw as I can get. So much from the sea and the best you can get. Organs are slimming to me.. no idea why! But you could try to eat lots of organs...(organs I always do raw)

I had 1 pound of raw elk meat for breakfast today with many tbsp raw coconut oil.. then for lunch 1 pound raw salmon with marinated mushrooms at work... and then for dinner avocado and oysters.. raw. And now I am going to warm me one cup of wildcaught fishbroth I made yesterday. I eat so much. But I am not fat. I am big and strong.  But I eat only the good stuff! Maybe you need the leptin reset way longer.. I need it still somehow, after more than one year. But I am a former bulemic.

Also I think curvier women are great! No need to be spider thin. Often we think we need to be like a catwalk model but that is not healthy at all... men like curves too... But sure, if the fat grows on your stomach it is not a good sign...?

You need to explore the cold dark and boring Audrey.. I tell you they are magic... never wish you had something else if that is the season where you are now.. but explore them with your whole heart instead, you are going to be greatly rewarded.

Inger, i love your response here. I can tell you just planted a new seed for me. Although intuitively I'm not "ready" for raw and never felt a MHS was right for me, something just landed; not sure what though. It will be interesting to explore.

I also love your suggestion to enjoy whatever season you are in. My favorite "tool" is to focus on what _is_ working rather than what is _not_ working. When I focus on that, more of it shows up in my life almost effortlessly

### curves64 — 2013-05-17T21:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86331>

I had a great experience yesterday. Now that it's spring here, I've been eating a ton of locally-grown veggies and a little fruit. Despite that, I couldn't figure out why I was still a little on the constipated side. Before I post my success, I have to confess, I haven't been much of a cook for most of my life. I was a typical big city urban girl in high heels and make up who ate out a lot -- so what I'm about to share might be obvious to you but was news to me. lol

I had been discarding the parts of the veggies that I don't typically eat like the stalks and tops. I got the idea that I could make a soup out of it because my building just added a compost bin and I saw how much I was tossing in it each week. So yesterday I used the veggie parts I would have discarded and made the most delicious broth ever! By the end of the day I was popping like crazy and I'm still popping today. That broth totally cured my constipation!

### caroline — 2013-05-19T01:14:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86436>

Since I am in an opposite season - I just thought I would tell you of my experiences going into spring last year.

I went down two sizes without even noticing what was happening - but my weight had gone up 1 K. After that my weight drifted down by about 4 K ..... I was really paying attention to what I was eating. My body comp changed and I can now look at myself naked .......that is huge for me .....and the real biggie - I don't think I look too bad!
I now wear shorts and a tank top .... First time in my life ever. I definitely got my life back ....better late than never!

Also - my weight was lower than it has ever been but I look healthy - you know how you usually look after you have dieted
\- kind of drawn and scrawny.....

### Jude — 2013-05-19T05:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86454>

Hi all,
Have also started the daily CT. Got with a daily immersion in my pool 10 days ago......having spoken to Ann on the big CT thread ..hi Ann and thanks for yr input.
Pool now 18 and managing 50mins total,including a 50 metre swim yesterday.That's my best so far.
Doing this mainly because of palpitations. Am seriously affected by our wireless....only option out here....gets my pulse rate up fairly quickly, so have to rapidly pull up all the posts, log off, read, get back onto bring up any I've missed...trying to limit time to 10..20min daily. Slow at typing so don't post much... wastes my searching time!
Guess my cold adaptation won't be as fast as those doing the 54 temps but was hoping staying in longer and actually swimming may make up for that?? Recovery time to warmth still taking about 30mins if I stay in the sun and sit on the warm bricks. Haven't measured my temp, but my skin isn't pink and feel fine. Looking forward to reading your results. Jude

### curves64 — 2013-05-19T08:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86462>

Day 1 -- CHECK!

I did get out to the beach today and into the water. If I hadn't made this commitment it surely would have been a day I would have turned around and gone home. Although the temp was maybe 60 and the water about 50, the winds were strong at about 20+. The good news was that when I was actually in the water the sand couldn't whip my body and stick to my coconut oil-covered skin lol

Tomorrow it turns out is Bay to Breakers. It's a big race they run here annually where people dress up in crazy costumes, run together as centipedes and'/or run half-dressed or naked. It's a huge event that ends at the beach making it hard to get near the water with 100,000 extra people in the city. I'm hoping that later on in the day I can find a bit of deserted beach somewhere -- if not, I guess it's the tub for me

### caroline — 2013-05-19T09:21:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86464>

Curves - I just see those changes in retrospect. I didn't realize I had gone down 2 sizes until a personal shopper at a store I frequent asked me why
I bought my clothes so big. Sounds ridiculous....We now refer to my wardrobe as ...Pre - Lisa ...
Or ....post - Lisa. She still has to grab things out of my hands and go back for smaller sizes.
It is only lately that I realize I am much firmer and don't mind looking at myself!
I had no trouble keeping my weight down although eating a lot.

But now is such a different story - so we really need to think about what happens when the seasons change and be prepared.

I have decided to do a short round on hcg and get this under control before it gets out of hand. The scary thing is - I am not eating things I shouldn't - I don't want to - I love how I was eating and being craving free.

I am definitely supporting all you guys doing the C/T - I will be walking in the ocean as usual and taking a quick dip in the rock pool. I am positive that is what changes the body comp.

Good luck everyone!

Ok finally got my act together and checking in...was embarrassed about my champagne and frozen yogurt bender...lol
We celebrated Mother's Day on Sunday since I was studying for a biology test last Sunday. I caved to grapefruit juice mimosas and a little arm twisting from the kids and we hit the new fro-yo place. Ugh.

Anyway, great CT yesterday almost an hour in tub with ice. Felt great, I love my CT! Will go again soon, after soccer practiced drop off. Good on food, trying some new things like a BPC around 2:30 to give me a little boost so I don't fall prey to that sleepy mindless sugar grab.

Glad to hear everyone is rocking it

Christi

### Inger — 2013-05-22T11:00:08+0300

The little girl that is 1 year old wanted to be with me naked in the river all the time..lol she liked the cold! Amazing!
It has been so warm here, 22 degree C. Too hot! Now it is getting cooler, like 15 degree C. I like that.. I hope the river cools off too, it was 16 degree F at its warmest.. it was not cooling even. I really like air temps at 15-18 C the best. Or even colder. And water as close to freezing as it gets, man it gives a different kick as those warm temps that are now..

### Inger — 2013-05-22T18:15:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86672>

Pinkie somewhere you asked if I eat no veggies?
I got so busy I forgot to answer but in winter I practically eat none. Now I have started to eat them once in a while, I love celery it is my all time favorite, I always loved it! I just eat it plain and raw or chopped in salads/food. But always raw. I like the chewiness of raw veggies. I do raw sauerkraut too once in a while. But I really do not eat huge amounts of them. Now as the wild greens are growing I eat a big handful every day in a smoothie, just with water or berries. I eat them plain too. I love dandelion flowers! Raw onions I love too.. I eat some almost every day.
The wild greens are so good as they are for free.. and very powerful for health too

### Inger — 2013-05-23T08:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-86714>

Audrey I think I eat high protein high fat...lol everything high huh! Ok carbs very low still. No fruit just a few veggies. But yes I need quite a lot protein to stay full long after breakfast. Lots of fat in the morning and less protein does not keep me full really... at least not coconut oil and butter. Maybe other animal fats would? That is possible for sure.
Now I usually do 300-400 gram meat and maybe 5-6 tbsp of fat for breakfast. Holds me satiated for maybe 5 hours or longer.. I never go hungry! I always eat. 3 times / day. No IF, no portion limitations.. if I feel I need more I eat more.

One summer I tried to eat only wild and raw.. no fat added.. for one week. I ate lean elk meat and berries and greens mainly. Man I lost weight like mad! 500 g / day! But after a few days I craved fat like crazy and my hair started to fall out........
I think I could do a rabbit starvation thing too and lose weight pretty quick but I do not want to lose my hair! And BTW, I am totally fine with my fat..lol I like how my body looks most days and it is strong and healthy, maybe no need to be skinny? At least I have a very flat stomach, no fat there really, so that is great.

### curves64 — 2013-05-28T19:56:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-87019>

so sorry for the silence everyone! I've been hyper-focused on a project for my business. So here's where I'm at re my commitment:

Day 5-NO CHECK. The winds were blustering at 35 mph at the beach and just when I was ready to get into the ice bath I had a surprise visitor.
Day 6-CHECK
Day 7-CHECK
Day 8-CHECK. The winds were blowing, so my BF and I thought we'd walk down the coast to find a non-windy spot but never found one. We braved the winds for 30 minutes in the water then ran for the car lol.
Day 9-CHECK Well sort of. Spot CTed my abs for 30 minutes.
Day 10-CHECK It was raining so had to hit the tub.

I've neither lost weight or inches but it's interesting to record the fluctuations from day-to-day.

I have a question about spot CTing. When I had the ice on my stomach for 15-20 minutes the following morning I had an ICE BURN!! Felt like a sun burn but was from the ice. Has anyone else experienced something like that?

### sjoshua — 2013-05-28T22:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-87025>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=86751)
>
> Santa Cruz must be lovely ...have a great time splashing around in the ocean!! naked if possible!! lol

It was lovely! I went down there on Friday and the weather was almost perfect... 75-80 and sunny out! The beach was quite busy, so a swim suit was required  It felt AWESOME to get in the water though. I'm guessing the temp was around 55-60 degrees but don't know exactly. The temp was a little warmer or on par with my showers. I swam and waded around just near/past the breaker where I could barely touch with my head above for around 30 minutes.

I think I started a trend when I got in, as a handful of others trickled in while I was basking... The few that stuck it out and didnt just dip their tows and run shared a head nod, a smirk and an unspoken bond as we passed each other in the water.

One of the most interesting take-aways for me was about 10-12 hours after. In the middle of the night I woke up with a pain in my lower back and a strong desire to touch the ground... or more specifically be back in the ocean lol. The location of the pain is a very tight spot and a known issue for me, but this was the first time I ever felt this sequence of events associated with it. I know one of the waves gave me a pretty good tumble and a "chiropractic adjustment" with some pops and cracks, but has anyone else experienced anything similar to this?

### curves64 — 2013-05-31T19:54:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/committing-to-a-one-month-ct-experiment.7055/post-87203>

It's been a crazy 5 days. I'm delivering content to a client in a new format and it required my intense concentration and focus. On Wednesday, I didn't move from my computer for 10 hours straight. Definitely not something I care to repeat too often but I made some interesting observations as a result:

1-Despite the intensity I didn't feel "stressed" in the typical fashion. In other words, I was handling the stress better than I ever remember
2-My mental focus did not decline at all during the 10 hour period
3-My attitude was great throughout

Having said that, by Thursday night, I could tell I had reached the limits of what I could do without resupplying and restocking my body with the photons and electrons it had become used to receiving.

The beach has been uncharacteristically super windy which makes it pretty unpleasant. Having said that, I managed a half an hour of earthing yesterday but couldn't really call it CT. Didn't get to the beach or the tub at all the day before.

I'm still considering myself committed to my CT experiment. I'm just going to dust myself off and get back to it. It's a good thing I gave up perfection a long time ago LOL

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T14:52:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/angelina-jolies-new-breasts-or-lack-of.7058/post-86080>

Cancer is not caused by genetic changes in our genome as most believe today. The genetic change we see in oncogenesis are the direct result of cancer formation to begin with by an altered cellular signaling gone awry. Once cancerous change occurs, cancers use glucose as their fuel source and alter the cells biochemistry to hijack the pathways. This hijacking then increases the leakiness of the first cytochrome in the mitochondria to ROS and mitochondria then die under the direction of glycolysis (carbs). This is why we should be advocating staying away from carbs in cancer cases. This was the source of my beef in the safe starches debate that broke out earlier this year, but was of course totally taken out of context by the paleo meme. Examine the poster from AHS 2012 I linked to earlier, done by a clinical oncologist (Caveman Doctor), which makes this point crystal clear. Dr. Otto Warburg wrote about this in 1928 and no one has proven him wrong as yet. The problem is cancer clinicians and researchers seem to have forgotten about this link to glucose and lactate. All of these things destroy normal cellular signaling and this can lead to cancer eventually if it left to continue.

MORE GEEKINESS: Another byproduct is acetaldehyde and it is related to formaldehyde this disrupts collagen production, fatty acid oxidation and blocks normal nerve functions. I see this problem as a spine surgeon because of the acceleration of disc degeneration in the spine. Orthopedic surgeons see it in the major joints. We are taught to replace those joints instead of fixing the real problem…….the altered gut. I got this message about 7 years ago and began to change the way I managed the diseases of the spine and brain. The ethanol and acetaldehyde will also help hasten the brain by allowing more inflammation into through the blood brain barrier and into the brain. When this occurs we see depression, the loss of DHA (fish oil fat), phospholipids, ceremides and sphingolipids in the brain and function slowly diminishes overtime. Protein folding becomes abnormal and things like Alzheimer’s, and Parkinson’s, and cerebral strokes become very common. When we lose DHA we tend to replace it with other PUFA’s that don’t have the “magic built” into its chemistry that DHA does. We outlined that briefly in Brain Gut 5. That was a pretty important blog post in retrospect. We also lose total body and brain iodine that protects our DHA from any oxidative attack. This diminishes the production of pro-resolution chemicals in the brain. They are called resolvins, lipoxins, and protectins. I mentioned these briefly in Brain Gut 5. They were discovered by an opthalmic researcher at LSU (Dr. Bazan) and this is where I first heard about them. Their job is to protect the most sensitive part of DHA’s PUFA structure from the oxidation of inflammation. They work in concert with iodine to protect the integrity in the areas of the synapse where nerve connections are made. This is where neural connections occur to control all the processes in our body. This allows us to control proper signaling. This is the source of all cancers. When inflammation is high signaling is lost in all types of neolithic diseases. Most diseases have an inflammatory brain component to them.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T14:54:22+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T18:52:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/angelina-jolies-new-breasts-or-lack-of.7058/post-86100>

Let me give you a small snippet of how cancer really works in the new world of epigenetics.......................We now know that in many cases, gene inactivation is NOT due to a bona fide DNA mutation like BRCA1 or 2. Instead, the **non-mutated gene** can be silenced by an epigenetic mechanism that involves histone deacetylation of the histone proteins surrounding active genes (euchromatin), as well as DNA methylation of a cytosine-rich region of promoter sites (“start location”) on the gene. This means any gene is capable of becoming a cancer.............These cytosine-rich areas are called CpG islands and the cytosine bases can be methylated by the enzyme DNMT. Did you know blue light and nnEMF cause hypermethylation by themselves? Remember Jolie is an actress and always in that light. Thousands of “good genes” must remain in the euchromatin state to allow for gene expression to occur. When histones are acetylated, transcription factors can reach the DNA because of the “open structure” of the histones. When the promoter region has a hypomethylated CpG island, the transcription factor can bind to the DNA promoter region. Transcription factors migrate into the nucleus in response to various cellular signaling pathways. are “silenced”, they cannot be turned on by cellular signals, even if the gene is not mutated.

My bet is Scott Kelly will come back from space and be hypermethylated compared to his brother left behind on Earth. If I am right, Jolie's decision to remove her breasts and ovaries will be shown to be a massive error in the future.

In English this states that your cellular terroir dictates to your genome and not vice versa. Go re-read levee one in the quilt..................there is Jolie's truth bomb.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T18:55:07+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-15T19:03:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/angelina-jolies-new-breasts-or-lack-of.7058/post-86104>

I have a levee about polyphenols and histones. Histones are proteins that bind to DNA and RNA to alter gene expression based upon the environmental triggers we find ourself in. Histone-based gene silencing is best understood as an imbalance in the equilibrium between histone acetylation and histone deacetylation. Acetylation comes from Acetyl Co Enz A from beta oxidation of fats. Deacteylation is more commonly found in oxidation of carbs and in glycation damaage................anyone connecting dots yet? The more EMF one exposes themselves too, the more deacteylation your DNA has................too!

What can help reverse this? Levee time!!!! Resveratrol and curcumin......et al: many plant polyphenols can inhibit the equilibrium both ways, since some of the polyphenols are both Histone DeAC inhibitors and Histone Acetylator inhibitors. HDAC inhibition is a way of preventing gene silencing of euchromatin, whereas HAT inhibition is primarily a way of preventing the activation and expression of repetitive DNA sequences, which should remain normally silent. The transcriptional activation of repetitive DNA is increasingly being studied by cancer and aging researchers since it seems to be the consequence of the global decline in DNA methylation that occurs with aging and cancer cases. Jolie docs need to tell her this..............whether she cuts off everything.............if her conditions of existence do not change..........the cancer will find another repeative DNA sequence to express and she will die from something else..............it is not about the genome it is however, 100% how the software runs the genome.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-17T00:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/angelina-jolies-new-breasts-or-lack-of.7058/post-86259>

Here is a big factoid Jolie and her docs dont seem to know: BRCA1 is most common gene that is mutated with hereditary breast cancer. However in sporadic cases of breast and ovarian cancer, the BRCA 1gene can be silenced by CpG island hypermethylation in the promoter site of this gene. Epigenetic silencing usually occurs due to promoter site (CpG island) methylation, but only 50% of genes have CpG islands. All genes can be epigenetically silenced by histone deacetylation, however. Another form of gene silencing is called polycomb protein silencing. Another form of silencing involves non-coding RNA called miRNA which prevent the translation of messenger RNA or increase the degradation of mRNA. DNA mutations also effectively turn off these genes, but mutations do not account for the number of cellular changes seen with cancer. This is why gene sequencing of cancer cells does not always reveal what genes are not working. The only way to truly figure this out is to do gene sequencing, epigenetic sequencing, and RNA profiling of cancer. With breast cancer, over 600 genes have been found to be silenced by histone acetylation which is an epigenetic mechanism. I hope she really likes those implants....................because they likely are not going to do her much good longer term.
