3b-Adiol

Atherosclerosis 2010-09-01
The androgen derivative
5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol
inhibits tumor necrosis factor @a and lipopolysaccharide induced inflammatory response in human endothelial cells and in mice aorta

> Abstract
> Background: An increasing body of evidence suggests that testosterone may exert beneficial effects against the development of atherosclerosis. These effects are thought to be the consequence of its conversion into estradiol and the activation of the estrogen receptors; however a direct role of androgens, such as dihydrotestosterone, has also been proposed. More recently, it has been shown that the transformation of the dihydrotestosterone to 5@a-androstane-3@a,17@b-diol (3@a-diol) and 5@a-androstane-3@b,17@b-diol (3@b-Adiol), generates two molecules unable to bind the androgen receptor, but with a high affinity for the estrogen receptors (ERs) in particular the @b isoform. As the actions of testosterone may result from the balance between androgenic and estrogenic molecules originating from its catabolism, we investigated the effects of the 3@b-Adiol on inflammatory responses in vitro in human endothelial cells and ex vivo in mice aortas. Methods and results: 3@b-Adiol reverts the pro-inflammatory gene expression pattern induced by TNF-@a in HUVECs as determined by a cDNA microrray approach. Q-real-time PCR and protein array approaches confirmed that TNF-@a-induced ICAM-1, VCAM-1 and ELAM-1 as well as MCP-1 and IL-6 induction was affected upon 3@b-Adiol pre-incubation. ICI 182780, an estrogen receptor antagonist and R,R-THC, an estrogen receptor @b antagonist, counteracted the effect of 3@b-Adiol while bicalutamide, an androgen receptor antagonist, had minor effects. 3@b-Adiol exerted a similar action on macrophages. Finally in castrated male mice, 3@b-Adiol significantly counteracted the LPS mediated mRNA induction of IL-6, ELAM-1and PECAM-1 in the aortas. Conclusion: 3@b-Adiol reverts in vitro the TNF-@a and LPS induced pro-inflammatory activation of endothelial cells and macrophages. 3@b-Adiol in vivo modulates the inflammatory response induced by LPS in the arterial vascular wall.

page 733 left middle

> In orthotopic xenograft experiments, mice were daily injected s.c. with 2.5 mg/kg 3b-Adiol in sesame oil or with corresponding amount of vehicle (sesame oil).
> Silastic pellets (Degania Silicone Ltd, Degania Bet, Israel) were prepared by cutting a silastic pipe (diameter 1.5 mm) into small segments of 1 cm long.
> These segments were filled with 3b-Adiol powder (or left empty in vehicle-treated mice), and closed to the edge with a double knot made by a surgical silk thread.
> Silastic pellets were then implanted s.c. on the left side of the mouse just behind the anterior leg using a trochar.

> Results 3b-Adiol regulation of PC cell growth In the initial set of experiments, we have measured the effects of increasing concentration of 3b-Adiol (0.1–10 nM) on AR-negative PC cell proliferation.
> In growth course experiments, 3b-Adiol significantly reduced (about 50% reduction at 10 nM, the maximal concentration tested), after 48-h treatment, the number of DU145, PC3, and PC3-Luc cells (Fig. 1A–C);
> the cell number reduction was due to inhibition of the proliferative rate of the cells as confirmed by thymidine

page 739 right bottom

> **It has also to be underlined that, on the basis of our results, the classical androgen depletion therapy widely utilized to treat PC should be re-evaluated.** The complete removal of androgens, which can be obtained using GnRH analog treatments, would also remove 3b-Adiol, the agent that exert a protection against PC cell proliferation and metastasis formation. It must be noted that the classical side effects of these drugs are generally related to estrogen deficiency that occurs as a result of treatment. These are often considered the results of E2 deprivation, but the estrogenic effects of 3b-Adiol might contribute to these systemic alterations (Freedland et al. 2009). Therefore, the protection of 3b-Adiol will be preserved using AR antagonists only.
> A retrospective analysis should thus be conducted to determine whether the androgen-insensitive PC may
> become more aggressive because of the protection offered by 3b-Adiol is eliminated by the GnRH analog-based therapy

/

### JanSz — 2013-05-28T02:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-cancer-how-to-treat.7171/post-86986>

[http://meridianvalleylab.com/1679-2](http://meridianvalleylab.com/1679-2)

The Anticancer Testosterone Metabolite 3β-Adiol

Published in: Townsend Letter

By. Dr. Jonathan v. Wright, MD

Age-related decline of testosterone in men is often seen as part of the “normal” aging process. Why, then, does the risk of prostate cancer increase with age? We could blame it on declining hormones, but not all men with declining (or elevated, for that matter) hormones get prostate cancer. For a long time, male hormone testing has been centered on testing only testosterone and sometimes (more frequently lately) free testosterone. One heavily researched area is the effect of dihydrotestosterone (DHT) on the prostate. But is testing DHT enough? The short answer: No.

Much of the DHT research has been associated with the patent medicine finasteride (Proscar, Propecia) and other related patent medicines. Finasteride greatly slows the metabolism of testosterone into the more potent (and more carcinogenic) metabolite DHT. (For the technically inclined, finasteride is a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor.)

Since DHT is generally agreed to be carcinogenic, the thought was that reducing the transformation of testosterone into DHT would reduce cancer risk. In the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial (PCPT), 18,882 men over age 55 years with normal prostate examinations and a PSA below 3.0 were randomly assigned finasteride or placebo for seven years.1

Slightly Lower Cancer Risk, Much Higher Killer Cancer Risk
The results were a surprise to the researchers, who found – as they expected – a lower rate of cancer in the finasteride group than in the placebo group (18.4% versus 24.4%). What surprised them was the considerably higher number of significantly aggressive cancers – for the technically inclined, higher Gleason scores – among those in the finasteride group with cancer versus those with cancer in the placebo group (37% versus 22.3%).

That’s why you never saw a television commercial about finasteride preventing prostate cancer. To be accurate it would have to say, “Take finasteride! It lowers your risk of prostate cancer, but if you do get prostate cancer, you’re more likely to die of it!”

The PCPT researchers concluded, “finasteride prevents or delays the appearance of prostate cancer, but this possible benefit and a reduced risk of urinary problems must be weighed against sexual side effects and the increased risk of high-grade prostate cancer.”

A separate meta-analysis published as a Cochrane Review found that 5a-reductase inhibitors, including finasteride, have inadequate evidence to say that these patent medicines reduce mortality, in terms of prostate cancer.2

It’s true that if DHT alone is considered, elevated levels of DHT might be thought to increase your prostate cancer risk, as DHT is a procarcinogenic (for the technically inclined, dedifferentiating) meta\*bolite.3,4

But Nature hasn’t provided DHT alone! If we take the metabolites of DHT into consideration, too, elevated DHT may or may not have this effect. One of these metabolites may actually offset or even reduce any DHT-increased risk. How does that happen?

Stop Suppressing Your Natural Defense
After testosterone is converted to DHT, DHT is in turn normally metabolized into a relatively smaller quantity of 5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol (abbreviated as 3a-Adiol), and a usually larger amount of 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol (abbreviated as 3b-Adiol). These same researchers also report that while nearly all the 3a-Adiol is converted back to DHT (which presumably makes 3a-Adiol a “pre-procarcinogen”), the 3b-Adiol does not convert back to 5a-DHT. Very importantly, they report that 3b-Adiol is an anticarcinogen (for the technically inclined, a redifferentiating agent) that activates estrogen receptor beta, an anticarcinogenic estrogen receptor present in large numbers in the prostate gland.5 (Estrogen receptor beta is present in many other tissues in both sexes, but that’s a topic to be explored at another time.)

What’s that? A testosterone metabolite that activates an estrogen receptor? A testosterone metabolite that doesn’t activate any androgen receptors at all? That’s right, and (very unfortunately for the men involved) my observation has been that many men who take finasteride to suppress procarcinogenic DHT end up suppressing their own anticarcinogenic 3b-Adiol even more, thus shifting the procarcinogen (DHT) to anticarcinogen (3b-Adiol) ratio toward a greater risk of cancer. I’ve also seen this occasionally with high doses of saw palmetto.

In a letter to the editor of the New England Journal of Medicine, Otabek Imamov, MD, et al. state, “\[DHT\] is the fulcrum in this balance. It suggests that finasteride, by blocking the conversion of testosterone to \[DHT\], inhibits the production of \[3b-Adiol\] thus suppressing \[the anticarcinogenic activity of\] ERb and preventing the \[re\]-differentiation of epithelium. This mechanism could account for the higher incidence of poorly differentiated tumors in the finasteride group in the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial.”6

A review in the Biology of Reproduction Journal states, “We believe that a higher incidence of low-differentiated \[more aggressive\] tumors in the finasteride-treated arm observed in the PCPT is caused by altering the normal differentiation of prostatic epithelium in the environment lacking the natural ERb ligand – \[3b-Adiol\].”7

Research has found some very specific things that 3b-Adiol does to inhibit prostate cancer growth. According to the title of a 2005 research report: “The androgen derivative 5a-androstane-3b, 17b-diol \[3b-Adiol\] inhibits prostate cancer cell migration through activation of the estrogen receptor beta subtype.”8 Other researchers reported that “3b-Adiol not only inhibits PC3-Luc cell \[a specific type of prostate cancer cell\] migratory properties, but also induces a broader antitumor phenotype \[type of cell\] by decreasing the proliferation \[growth\] rate, increasing cell adhesion \[cancer cells don't "stick" as normal cells do\] and reducing invasive capabilities in vitro.”9 But these researchers went beyond test-tubes to living mice, writing “In vivo, continuous administration of 3b-Adiol reduces growth of established tumors and counteracts metastasis formation when PC3-Luc cells are engrafted subcutaneously in nude mice or are injected into the prostate.”

The conclusion to this research article was very encouraging: “Since 3b-Adiol has no androgenic activity, and cannot be converted to androgenic compounds, the effects here described entail a novel potential application of this agent against human PC.”9 A novel potential application of 3b-Adiol, a totally natural human testosterone, against human prostate cancer! Where are the headlines? This article was published in 2010!

For the really technically inclined, here are several “mechanisms of action” of 3b-Adiol, all of which come from stimulation of estrogen receptor beta:

Â§ repression of VEGF-A (vascular endothelial growth factor A) expression
Â§ destabilization of HIF-1a (hypoxia-inducible factor 1a)
Â§ reduction of “Snail1″ (can’t identify this acronym) relocation from the cytoplasm to the nucleus of cancer cells

According to the researchers who published the above mechanisms of action, “… high Gleason grade cancers … exhibit significantly more HIF-1a and VEGF-A and Snail1 nuclear localization compared to low Gleason grade cancers.”10

cont next post

### JanSz — 2013-05-28T02:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-cancer-how-to-treat.7171/post-86987>

continuation from post #3

Procarcinogenic and Anticarcinogenic Metabolites
The testosterone Â® DHT Â® 3b-Adiol metabolic pathway is very similar in its pro- and anticancer effects to the estrogen metabolic pathway estrone (E1) Â® 16a-hydroxyestrone (16a-OHE1) Â® estriol (E3). While 16a-OHE1 has a “good side” and helps protect against osteoporosis, its bad side is that too much 16-OHE1 increases breast and prostate cancer risk.11-14 But like DHT, which also increases cancer risk, 16a-OHE1 is transformed into the anticarcinogenic “good-guy” (more precisely, “good-girl”) estriol. Just as we want to promote the formation of more estriol to “tilt” the balance of a woman’s estrogens in an anticarcinogenic direction, we want to make sure that 3b-Adiol is more abundant than DHT, to “tilt” the balance of a man’s androgens in an anticarcinogenic direction.

Until the recent research about 3b-Adiol became available, when physicians saw an elevated DHT test, they frequently recommended reducing the man’s testosterone dose – even if he didn’t feel as well with that lower dose – because of understandable concern about higher cancer risk. There has been no test for 3b-Adiol, no way for men using testosterone replacement or the physicians prescribing it to assess the pro- and anticarcinogenic balance of major testosterone metabolites.

The Test That Could Save Your Life
Now that test is available. Thanks to the work of Dawn Huo, PhD, at Meridian Valley Laboratory, you or your physician can request a blood test for testosterone, DHT, 3a-Adiol, and 3b-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol), check the relative proportions of each, and have a more clear idea about your cancer risk. Termed “testosterone metabolites,” this blood serum test also measures androstenedione (spelled almost but not quite exactly the same way as “androstanediol”), another androgen metabolite, and 3a-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol. (Extensive quality control testing by Dr. Huo has shown that presently available tests for urine DHT are far less accurate than serum DHT.)

Remember, both 3b-Adiol and 3a-Adiol are metabolites of DHT, but researchers have found that almost all 3a-Adiol turns back into DHT (thus making it a procarcinogenic precursor), while none of the 3b-Adiol – which stimulates estrogen receptor beta (ERb), and is entirely anticarcinogenic – can “back-metabolize” into DHT again.6

Theoretically, if a man’s 3b-Adiol level is greater than the sum of his DHT plus 3a-Adiol (3b-Adiol > DHT + 3a-Adiol), his “testosterone metabolite ratio” should be anticarcinogenic. Please keep in mind that at this time, the testosterone metabolite ratio is theoretical, supported by inference from considerable scientific research, but not absolutely proven. Unlike the 2-hydroxyestrogen/16a- hydroxyestrogen ratio (“2/16″ ratio), which has been proved to predict lower and higher cancer risk in premenopausal women, or the estrogen quotient (EQ), the testosterone metabolite ratio has not yet been studied in groups of men over time, which is necessary to absolutely prove a theory.

Over the years, I’ve had occasion to observe the multiple adverse effects of patent medications on normal metabolism. My observation has been that when men are given a patent medication that slows down the metabolization of testosterone into DHT, much more often than not, 3b-Adiol is suppressed even further, so the testosterone metabolite ratio is rebalanced in a procarcinogenic direction. As Professor Imamov explained, this is very likely why the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial found a significantly higher proportion of highly aggressive cancers in the men who despite taking the patent medicine finasteride still developed prostate cancer.7 Note that, in addition to finasteride (Proscar, Propecia), dutasteride (Avodart) can have this effect, as can very large quantities of saw palmetto. And as “bonus points,” many men who take these patent medicines also have diminished libido, and varying degrees of erectile dysfunction (ED).

By contrast, I usually recommended quantities of zinc, GLA (gamma-linolenic acid), and other natural inhibitors of the testosterone Â® DHT transformation are “gentler”; their use rarely results in a procarcinogenic testosterone metabolite ratio.

Three Reasons to Test for 3b-Adiol and the Procarcinogenic Estrogen Metabolites.
I realize that despite my best attempts to explain testosterone metabolite testing in plain English, the preceding is highly technical. Please consider testosterone metabolite testing if you’re:

1\. using testosterone replacement therapy. For unknown reasons, “replacement” testosterone metabolizes into DHT more than internally secreted testosterone. It’s best to make sure that if this happens, you have a greater quantity of 3b-Adiol than DHT + 3a-Adiol;

2\. taking Proscar, Propecia, Avodart, or very large amounts – usually over 500 milligrams – of saw palmetto daily. Particularly with the use of these patent medications, the “testosterone metabolite ratio” is most often decidedly procarcinogenic;

3\. a man whose family has a history of prostate cancer. In this case, you want to check for estrogen metabolites too, as a low “2/16″ estrogen ratio has been associated with higher prostate cancer risk.12

The testosterone metabolite test can be drawn at any lab and sent from there by the usual means to the Meridian Valley Laboratory ([www.meridianvalleylab.com](http://www.meridianvalleylab.com) or 425-271-8689 ) for performance and reporting

Potential Endogenous Stimulation of 3b-Adiol
For several months in 2011, compounding pharmacies were able to supply “3b-Adiol” at a relatively reasonable prices. But – according to compounding pharmacists – a certain agency of los federales began to interfere with freedom of trade. The price quickly escalated, and then 3b-Adiol became entirely unavailable. Surprised, anyone?

Fortunately, researchers are reporting possibilities for stimulating the natural endogenous biosynthesis of 3b-Adiol with natural substances which stimulate 3b-HSD and/or 17b-HSD, the enzymes that convert 5a-DHT into 3b-Adiol. But there’s a caution: Although these studies are theoretically promising, none of them have as yet actually measured “before and after” quantities of 3b-Adiol itself, but rather activity and/or quantity of the enzymes that “lead to” 3b-Adiol. Until this research has been done, the best alternative is measuring the “before and after” levels in individuals, especially individuals found to personally have low 3b-Adiol levels. (In my own practice, I’ve observed that several of the items below have been associated with improved 3b-Adiol levels in individuals, but it’s too early to report that any one is reliably associated with improvement in low 3b-Adiol levels.)

Let’s start with (no kidding) coconut oil and olive oil. In 2008 and 2009, researchers reported that these two oils, used separately, significantly stimulated the activity of 3b-HSD and 17b-HSD, as well as significantly raising testosterone levels in experimental animals, while grapeseed oil and soy oil did not have any significant effect.15,16

Then there’s our old “male health” standby, zinc. Studies in male rats demonstrated that zinc deficiency decreased 3b-HSD activity. Zinc deficiency also was associated with a very significant reduction in testosterone itself.17

Hypothyroidism and “subclinical” hypothyroidism are relatively common. Here’s another reason to be ever-vigilant for these problems: T3 stimulates 3b-HSD type 2.18 Although this might suggest that T3 may stimulate 3b-Adiol, there are as yet no publications exploring this possibility.

However, it’s easy enough to check in any one individual with lower than desirable 3b-Adiol.

3b-HSD also requires NAD (niacinamide adenine dinucleotide), but once again there are no studies yet linking NADH supplementation with improved levels of 3b-Adiol.19
In a study of adrenal cell activity, lithium was reported to increase synthesis of 3b-HSD type 2. (Obviously the adrenals and testes are entirely different, but the 3b-HSD enzyme is the same enzyme in both areas).

Lastly (for now) all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) has been shown to increase 3b-HSD type 2.20 As too much ATRA can become toxic, this one is available only by prescription.

> Results 3b-Adiol regulation of PC cell growth In the initial set of experiments, we have measured the effects of increasing concentration of 3b-Adiol (0.1–10 nM) on AR-negative PC cell proliferation.
> In growth course experiments, 3b-Adiol significantly reduced (about 50% reduction at 10 nM, the maximal concentration tested), after 48-h treatment, the number of DU145, PC3, and PC3-Luc cells (Fig. 1A–C);
> the cell number reduction was due to inhibition of the proliferative rate of the cells as confirmed by thymidine

page 739 right bittom

> **It has also to be underlined that, on the basis of our results, the classical androgen depletion therapy widely utilized to treat PC should be re-evaluated.** The complete removal of androgens, which can be obtained using GnRH analog treatments, would also remove 3b-Adiol, the agent that exert a protection against PC cell proliferation and metastasis formation. It must be noted that the classical side effects of these drugs are generally related to estrogen deficiency that occurs as a result of treatment. These are often considered the results of E2 deprivation, but the estrogenic effects of 3b-Adiol might contribute to these systemic alterations (Freedland et al. 2009). Therefore, the protection of 3b-Adiol will be preserved using AR antagonists only.
> A retrospective analysis should thus be conducted to determine whether the androgen-insensitive PC may
> become more aggressive because of the protection offered by 3b-Adiol is eliminated by the GnRH analog-based therapy

/

### Destiny — 2013-06-25T14:04:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-cancer-how-to-treat.7171/post-88885>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=86987)
>
> continuation from post #3
>
>
> Procarcinogenic and Anticarcinogenic Metabolites
> The testosterone Â® DHT Â® 3b-Adiol metabolic pathway is very similar in its pro- and anticancer effects to the estrogen metabolic pathway estrone (E1) Â® 16a-hydroxyestrone (16a-OHE1) Â® estriol (E3). While 16a-OHE1 has a â€œgood sideâ€ and helps protect against osteoporosis, its bad side is that too much 16-OHE1 increases breast and prostate cancer risk.11-14 But like DHT, which also increases cancer risk, 16a-OHE1 is transformed into the anticarcinogenic â€œgood-guyâ€ (more precisely, â€œgood-girlâ€) estriol. Just as we want to promote the formation of more estriol to â€œtiltâ€ the balance of a womanâ€™s estrogens in an anticarcinogenic direction, we want to make sure that 3b-Adiol is more abundant than DHT, to â€œtiltâ€ the balance of a manâ€™s androgens in an anticarcinogenic direction.
>
> Until the recent research about 3b-Adiol became available, when physicians saw an elevated DHT test, they frequently recommended reducing the manâ€™s testosterone dose â€“ even if he didnâ€™t feel as well with that lower dose â€“ because of understandable concern about higher cancer risk. There has been no test for 3b-Adiol, no way for men using testosterone replacement or the physicians prescribing it to assess the pro- and anticarcinogenic balance of major testosterone metabolites.
>
> The Test That Could Save Your Life
> Now that test is available. Thanks to the work of Dawn Huo, PhD, at Meridian Valley Laboratory, you or your physician can request a blood test for testosterone, DHT, 3a-Adiol, and 3b-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol), check the relative proportions of each, and have a more clear idea about your cancer risk. Termed â€œtestosterone metabolites,â€ this blood serum test also measures androstenedione (spelled almost but not quite exactly the same way as â€œandrostanediolâ€), another androgen metabolite, and 3a-Adiol (full name: 5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol. (Extensive quality control testing by Dr. Huo has shown that presently available tests for urine DHT are far less accurate than serum DHT.)
>
> Remember, both 3b-Adiol and 3a-Adiol are metabolites of DHT, but researchers have found that almost all 3a-Adiol turns back into DHT (thus making it a procarcinogenic precursor), while none of the 3b-Adiol â€“ which stimulates estrogen receptor beta (ERb), and is entirely anticarcinogenic â€“ can â€œback-metabolizeâ€ into DHT again.6
>
> Theoretically, if a manâ€™s 3b-Adiol level is greater than the sum of his DHT plus 3a-Adiol (3b-Adiol > DHT + 3a-Adiol), his â€œtestosterone metabolite ratioâ€ should be anticarcinogenic. Please keep in mind that at this time, the testosterone metabolite ratio is theoretical, supported by inference from considerable scientific research, but not absolutely proven. Unlike the 2-hydroxyestrogen/16a- hydroxyestrogen ratio (â€œ2/16″ ratio), which has been proved to predict lower and higher cancer risk in premenopausal women, or the estrogen quotient (EQ), the testosterone metabolite ratio has not yet been studied in groups of men over time, which is necessary to absolutely prove a theory.
>
> Over the years, Iâ€™ve had occasion to observe the multiple adverse effects of patent medications on normal metabolism. My observation has been that when men are given a patent medication that slows down the metabolization of testosterone into DHT, much more often than not, 3b-Adiol is suppressed even further, so the testosterone metabolite ratio is rebalanced in a procarcinogenic direction. As Professor Imamov explained, this is very likely why the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial found a significantly higher proportion of highly aggressive cancers in the men who despite taking the patent medicine finasteride still developed prostate cancer.7 Note that, in addition to finasteride (Proscar, Propecia), dutasteride (Avodart) can have this effect, as can very large quantities of saw palmetto. And as â€œbonus points,â€ many men who take these patent medicines also have diminished libido, and varying degrees of erectile dysfunction (ED).
>
> By contrast, I usually recommended quantities of zinc, GLA (gamma-linolenic acid), and other natural inhibitors of the testosterone Â® DHT transformation are â€œgentlerâ€; their use rarely results in a procarcinogenic testosterone metabolite ratio.
>
> Three Reasons to Test for 3b-Adiol and the Procarcinogenic Estrogen Metabolites.
> I realize that despite my best attempts to explain testosterone metabolite testing in plain English, the preceding is highly technical. Please consider testosterone metabolite testing if youâ€™re:
>
> 1\. using testosterone replacement therapy. For unknown reasons, â€œreplacementâ€ testosterone metabolizes into DHT more than internally secreted testosterone. Itâ€™s best to make sure that if this happens, you have a greater quantity of 3b-Adiol than DHT + 3a-Adiol;
>
> 2\. taking Proscar, Propecia, Avodart, or very large amounts â€“ usually over 500 milligrams â€“ of saw palmetto daily. Particularly with the use of these patent medications, the â€œtestosterone metabolite ratioâ€ is most often decidedly procarcinogenic;
>
> 3\. a man whose family has a history of prostate cancer. In this case, you want to check for estrogen metabolites too, as a low â€œ2/16″ estrogen ratio has been associated with higher prostate cancer risk.12
>
> The testosterone metabolite test can be drawn at any lab and sent from there by the usual means to the Meridian Valley Laboratory ([www.meridianvalleylab.com](http://www.meridianvalleylab.com) or 425-271-8689 ) for performance and reporting
>
> Potential Endogenous Stimulation of 3b-Adiol
> For several months in 2011, compounding pharmacies were able to supply â€œ3b-Adiolâ€ at a relatively reasonable prices. But â€“ according to compounding pharmacists â€“ a certain agency of los federales began to interfere with freedom of trade. The price quickly escalated, and then 3b-Adiol became entirely unavailable. Surprised, anyone?
>
> Fortunately, researchers are reporting possibilities for stimulating the natural endogenous biosynthesis of 3b-Adiol with natural substances which stimulate 3b-HSD and/or 17b-HSD, the enzymes that convert 5a-DHT into 3b-Adiol. But thereâ€™s a caution: Although these studies are theoretically promising, none of them have as yet actually measured â€œbefore and afterâ€ quantities of 3b-Adiol itself, but rather activity and/or quantity of the enzymes that â€œlead toâ€ 3b-Adiol. Until this research has been done, the best alternative is measuring the â€œbefore and afterâ€ levels in individuals, especially individuals found to personally have low 3b-Adiol levels. (In my own practice, Iâ€™ve observed that several of the items below have been associated with improved 3b-Adiol levels in individuals, but itâ€™s too early to report that any one is reliably associated with improvement in low 3b-Adiol levels.)
>
> Letâ€™s start with (no kidding) coconut oil and olive oil. In 2008 and 2009, researchers reported that these two oils, used separately, significantly stimulated the activity of 3b-HSD and 17b-HSD, as well as significantly raising testosterone levels in experimental animals, while grapeseed oil and soy oil did not have any significant effect.15,16
>
> Then thereâ€™s our old â€œmale healthâ€ standby, zinc. Studies in male rats demonstrated that zinc deficiency decreased 3b-HSD activity. Zinc deficiency also was associated with a very significant reduction in testosterone itself.17
>
> Hypothyroidism and â€œsubclinicalâ€ hypothyroidism are relatively common. Hereâ€™s another reason to be ever-vigilant for these problems: T3 stimulates 3b-HSD type 2.18 Although this might suggest that T3 may stimulate 3b-Adiol, there are as yet no publications exploring this possibility.
>
> However, itâ€™s easy enough to check in any one individual with lower than desirable 3b-Adiol.
>
> 3b-HSD also requires NAD (niacinamide adenine dinucleotide), but once again there are no studies yet linking NADH supplementation with improved levels of 3b-Adiol.19
> In a study of adrenal cell activity, lithium was reported to increase synthesis of 3b-HSD type 2. (Obviously the adrenals and testes are entirely different, but the 3b-HSD enzyme is the same enzyme in both areas).
>
> Lastly (for now) all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) has been shown to increase 3b-HSD type 2.20 As too much ATRA can become toxic, this one is available only by prescription.
>
>
> \======================================
>
> [http://www.questdiagnostics.com/testcenter/BUOrderInfo.action?tc=16808P&labCode=SJC](http://www.questdiagnostics.com/testcenter/BUOrderInfo.action?tc=16808P&labCode=SJC)
>
> **
> 3a-Androstanediol Glucuronide (3-Alpha Diol G) (5276X)
>
> \===============
> LabCorp
>
> Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154
> **
>
>
> ......
>
>
>

>
> //

# Thread: Not quite sure if I'm Leptin Resistant - A few questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-quite-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant-a-few-questions.7173/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 10

### fireforlunch — 2013-05-28T06:09:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-quite-sure-if-im-leptin-resistant-a-few-questions.7173/post-87000>

Was just reading Jack's epic blog post on leptin resistance:
http://www.jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/

Just reading the section on determining if you're leptin resistant where he says:

> How do I determine if I am leptin resistant? Remember, you can be LR (leptin resistant) if youâ€™re fat or skinny. If youâ€™re overweight by more than 30lbs, it is a lock you have some degree of LR. If youâ€™re underweight by 20 lbs, you are likely LR, too. If you had an eating disorder, youâ€™re likely suffering from a serious leptin issue.
>
> The easiest test is to look in the mirror. The mirror does not lie and it is really cheap. For those people who still canâ€™t be sure after peeking in the mirror, you can order some blood tests. My favorite is the HS CRP (highly sensitive C-Reactive protein) and the reverse T3 tests (but there are others). They are accurate in over 90% of cases.
>
> My next favorite test is the salivary cortisol test, along with at least two of the following: HDL level, serum ferritin, homocysteine, fasting triglycerides, fasting insulin, or post-prandial glucose readings done every 15 minutes for one hour after eating. (Read my Hormone 101 blog for details.)

He doesn't really give details on what to look for when ordering tests.

I'm not overweight - Male, 190 around 15% bf

I've had some of those tests and they come back normal. My HDL is 87, my fasting triglycerides are 75 and fasting insulin is 87.

I'm a firefighter so this may account for some of my issues (mainly sleep).

For the last 9 months my sleep has been terrible with waking multiple times per night, sometimes hard to go back to sleep but usually not.

I do crossfit about 5 times per week along with a paleo diet.

In addition to poor sleep, it seems like I'm always hungry (sometimes I give in sometimes I don't but I pretty don't eat processed sugar) and I have been having odd muscle pains (but I think that might be because of the poor sleep).

My energy has been terrible since the poor sleep but it was never great even before my sleep tanked even with eating strict paleo.

So what else can I do to figure out if I'm leptin resistant or should I just go for the reset considering the info I have provided.

If I do go for the reset there is one main problem that I may have a hard time following: No exercise.

At the fire station we sometimes go about and play football for roughly 3 hrs/week total. Not tackling but quite a bit of sprinting.

I would really lose face if I sat out.

**Thanks for any input you have.**

John

### cinnamon — 2013-05-29T23:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-breast-cancer.7185/post-87092>

She lost me when she started talking about still using her cell phone quite a bit. That's nuts!

### kleinb87 — 2013-06-01T17:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-breast-cancer.7185/post-87255>

That is crazy that she still thinks that using the phone 24/7 is okay. Speaking of cancer, I have been in Chicago for a few days for business. I met an oncologist at dinner last night who is in town for ASCO conference. We began to talk about health. She was amazed with my progress but still did not get the connection. I think I had given her a heart attack when I told her how much butter and coconut oil I eat. She couldn't question the results, but immediately tried to get me to market big pharma drugs as I do marketing. However, she still did not understand my whole dilemma of how the drug only masks the problem and how I could not do this based on a conflict of opinion. Her only response was "if the money's right, you would do it, right?" She thought I was a unique case and had no idea about the issues that were all caused via leptin and mitochondria problems. She couldn't deny my results as I have all of the data to prove everything (I'm that patient who has learned to memorize their labs from hs-crp, vit d, dhea, on down). Had I not found Jack, I could have been like any of her other patients that she sees that just go with the doctor's orders without questioning. As that is their only option to live or so they believe. She even tried to get me to go onto a cancer drug for methylation. It flew over her head that I have already bypassed the methylation pathways.

I didn't think I would end up speaking about biochemistry and health during dinner, but it was eye opening at how close minded some people really are when it comes to advances in medicine. They only know what they believe and don't want to study more than is necessary.

This is what we are up against. People are no longer standing up for what they believe in and are being bought by the power of money. Once we get away from that, there is a world of possibilities.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-01T18:25:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-breast-cancer.7185/post-87256>

Soon in the next few weeks of blogs you will see why doctors are like this......because the scientists use reductionism without observation. I will explain it in detail.

# Thread: Melanin and Photoelectric effect?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GlenPDQ — 2013-05-29T23:49:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/post-87098>

Any ideas how melanin figures into the photoelectric effect? I know people with more melanin make less vitamin D, but is there some beneficial interaction with melanin, sunlight, and electrons? I found out that phenylalanine increases melanin and some wheels started turning. Does melanin make any uncoupling proteins?

### GlenPDQ — 2013-05-30T00:04:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/post-87100>

After some more searching I found info that once pheomelanin in women and eumelanin in men are raised to a sufficiently excited level, the excess energy is assumed to be used by the cell for chemical processes and to regulate cell conditions.

One could say people with more melanin might produce less vitamin D, but the tradeoff is they get more energy for the cell to use.

I wonder if melanin can dampen EMF..

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-30T03:28:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/post-87113>

Melanin does dampen EMF........The more photons and electrons you get the tanner you get. Why? Your body is trying to tell you we got enough on some of the semiconductors.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-30T03:31:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/post-87114>

Every cell in the living matrix needs photons when the system is somehow traumatized.......the skin/eye is one way we deliver it. We also sense Electromagnetism and the resonance and vibrations via hydrogen bonding networks and the amide bonds in collagen.......there is a lot more to this. Melanin creates a cycle for water disassociation and reassociation in mitochondria.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-05-30T03:35:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-photoelectric-effect.7193/post-87116>

Thanks for the reply, Jack. It makes me happy to be able to connect some dots. I'm wondering if the additional energy generated by melanin for the cells might be one of the ways darker skinned tropicals' metabolisms cope with additional heat and starches.

I recall there is a black fungus that grows on the walls of nuclear reactors. The black melanin it produces protects it from the radiation.

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2013-05-30T19:55:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-and-sunshine.7202/post-87148>

Saw this posted today on f'book...

[http://www.helpforadd.com/2009/february.htm](http://www.helpforadd.com/2009/february.htm)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-05-30T22:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-and-sunshine.7202/post-87153>

its called sunshine!!! LOL Amazon the story they create around why the sun might be good for us.

### Dextery — 2013-05-30T23:11:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-and-sunshine.7202/post-87156>

Many of you are not old enough to remember extreme sun phobia and how we looked down at the sun worshipers at the swimming pool or at the beach.

We just knew they were going to die from melenoma. I knew some of those folks and those sun worshipers were a healthy lot but we never used critical thinking to ask why. Many overdid the sun to the point of leather skin but I don't recall them getting melanoma. Those that use sunscreen...as the dermatologists say... I understand it are more prone for skin cancer
And not one of the sun worshipers had ADHD or ADD. Is our sun phobia a major contributor of ADHD/ADD? It seems that the emphasis is placed on already dx cases of ADHD/ADD. What about sunshine right out of the womb? And what about Mom getting sunshine during pregnancy?

It sure looks like photons and adequate D3 levels win out over squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma and ADHD/ADD.

# Thread: Still Hungry?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LRichard12 — 2013-05-31T12:55:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87178>

Hi I'm up to 70 grams of protein, 66 grams of fat, 4 carbs in the morning and I'm still hungry within an hour or so of eating. Should I up the protein? I don't want to force glucogenisis and kick myself out of ketosis. Any suggestions?

### Dali Dula — 2013-05-31T13:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87180>

More fat. Eat as much fat as you want. Eat fat until your body tells you it's enough. You should not be hungry at least till mid day or evening meal.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-01T00:42:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87227>

Thanks for the advice Dali and for the affirmation Dr Kruse! My concern is that after eating 70 grams of protein and 66 grams of fat at nearly 1000 cal I could have easily eaten twice that with my appetite. Is there a concern if I eat too much? Will I gain weight?

### Dali Dula — 2013-06-01T14:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87248>

Listen to your body. Eat fat and protein upon rising till your body says stop. Not your mind, your body. Counting calories is not necessary if you follow the protocols.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-01T16:48:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87250>

Eat what it takes to destroy your cravings.......eventually when your electron density of current accross you inner mitochondrial membrane catches up......you will begin to calorie restrict naturally.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-01T16:49:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87251>

Right now you cant do this because you can not quantum tunnel well in your mitochondria......this is why your appetite is off the chain.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-01T17:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-87253>

[http://vimeo.com/67017515](http://vimeo.com/67017515)

### fireforlunch — 2013-07-19T15:07:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90363>

When i first started i felt the same way. I encreased my fat.

Some mornings i would eat over 120g of fat

After a couple weeks my hunger subsided.

Stay with it and it will get better

> [LRichard12 said:](/goto/post?id=87227)
>
> Thanks for the advice Dali and for the affirmation Dr Kruse! My concern is that after eating 70 grams of protein and 66 grams of fat at nearly 1000 cal I could have easily eaten twice that with my appetite. Is there a concern if I eat too much? Will I gain weight?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-19T19:45:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90383>

nope.........Somedays I eat 7K calories......and I am still weight stable. four days ago what I ate just floored my waiter........

### Hemming — 2013-07-19T21:06:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90390>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=87180)
>
> More fat. Eat as much fat as you want. Eat fat until your body tells you it's enough. You should not be hungry at least till mid day or evening meal.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=87191)
>
> bingo.......

Thanks, this is something I can use too even though I might not have the same problem as LRichard12. What I've also noticed is that it is very difficult to gain weight despite eating a lot of fat (even though I probably should be eating even more).

### Inger — 2013-07-19T22:09:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90397>

I do that too.. I can sometimes eat so much I almost get scared! The other day I had eaten 1 pound of pollock and lots of EVOO for dinner.. and then I went to buy some food.. and I felt I need some food again?! I know weird but I craved this french raw sheep cheese.. so I buy it and some organic red pepper. On my way home I sat down by the road in the grass barefeet and ate the whole 200 gram cheese and 1 whole pepper with it. Man it was good! I bet I had 4000 calories that day. Often I eat at least 6 big spoons of coconut oil for breakfast, or even more.. with my meat. I do not understand but I do not gain weight either lol

### Hemming — 2013-07-19T22:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90400>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=90397)
>
> I do that too.. I can sometimes eat so much I almost get scared! The other day I had eaten 1 pound of pollock and lots of EVOO for dinner.. and then I went to buy some food.. and I felt I need some food again?! I know weird but I craved this french raw sheep cheese.. so I buy it and some organic red pepper. On my way home I sat down by the road in the grass barefeet and ate the whole 200 gram cheese and 1 whole pepper with it. Man it was good! I bet I had 4000 calories that day. Often I eat at least 6 big spoons of coconut oil for breakfast, or even more.. with my meat. I do not understand but I do not gain weight either lol

Very interesting, thank you for sharing that. I'm currently trying to convince myself that it's okay to eat (especially protein I have an irrational fear about) so it's good to hear these stories.

Btw, do you know the name of the cheese?

### Inger — 2013-07-20T08:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90418>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=90400)
>
> Very interesting, thank you for sharing that. I'm currently trying to convince myself that it's okay to eat (especially protein I have an irrational fear about) so it's good to hear these stories.
>
> Btw, do you know the name of the cheese?

Oh yes. Roquefort. Hope I spelled it right.  Very expensive here, 52 € / kg. But oh so good.... especially with some sweet red pepper or maybe cucumber.. or apples but they are no good for me so I never eat them.

Hemming, I have been eating massive amounts of protein for years now.. and I am fine! I do have been thinking to limit it a bit now and eat more fat. Look, it is not unusual for me to eat 1 kg lean protein meat + fish in one day.. like 250 grams lean elk meat, and 800 gram of pollock (very lean too) and lots of additional fat with it for sure. But I do think this is a little too much for me.. I am 166 cm and weigh maybe 54 kg or so. I do not think it is maybe good to eat so much protein rest of my life..haha.. but it has not killed me at least! Last days I have had more fat and less protein and some carbs too, lots of wild berries! Sometimes a ripe, red organic grapefruit with my breakfast.

I have had some challenges.. and I dunno but they always make me eat sooo much? Maybe I need something in the food. At least I am not stressing over it. When I feel I need to eat much much I do. I just eat the good stuff. When I am in love.. or very happy without too many weird challenges and issues to resolve I eat less, at least that is how it feels.

### Hemming — 2013-07-20T09:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90421>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=90418)
>
> Oh yes. Roquefort. Hope I spelled it right.  Very expensive here, 52 € / kg. But oh so good.... especially with some sweet red pepper or maybe cucumber.. or apples but they are no good for me so I never eat them.
>
> Hemming, I have been eating massive amounts of protein for years now.. and I am fine! I do have been thinking to limit it a bit now and eat more fat. Look, it is not unusual for me to eat 1 kg lean protein meat + fish in one day.. like 250 grams lean elk meat, and 800 gram of pollock (very lean too) and lots of additional fat with it for sure. But I do think this is a little too much for me.. I am 166 cm and weigh maybe 54 kg or so. I do not think it is maybe good to eat so much protein rest of my life..haha.. but it has not killed me at least! Last days I have had more fat and less protein and some carbs too, lots of wild berries! Sometimes a ripe, red organic grapefruit with my breakfast.
>
> I have had some challenges.. and I dunno but they always make me eat sooo much? Maybe I need something in the food. At least I am not stressing over it. When I feel I need to eat much much I do. I just eat the good stuff. When I am in love.. or very happy without too many weird challenges and issues to resolve I eat less, at least that is how it feels.

Inger, thanks a lot for reply. I've really been struggling with allowing myself to eat to the point that I feel truly full. Many nights I dream about food simply because I'm hungry I think. It's really a relief to hear your experiences. Today at breakfast I've been trying to eat more and I do feel better but there is still some way to go. I weigh 2kg less than you but is more than 10cm higher so I guess I shouldn't be that scared of consuming the same amount of protein as you

That does seem rather pricey for Roquefort, in Denmark it's around 40/kg.

### Inger — 2013-07-20T09:40:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90422>

Hemming,you know I do not think fish protein will hurt you, raw or cooked gently. I do think lots and lots of red meat fried or such is not good. But I do not eat that.. so if your protein source is good I think no need to worry. Just try to make your main protein source from the sea, and it will only do you good.

I can get a little cheaper Roquefort here too.. the prepacked small packets but I like the pieces cut from the big cheese better. Also always check that is it unpasteurized! I do think it always is with Roquefort, though. I never eat pasteurized cheese ever. I do not even like the taste..lol
Finland is pretty expensive with foods. The prices has gone up 20% last years... that is pretty much! And the payments for work is pretty much constant. The only thing I can afford is.. food..lol That is why I am leaving to work in Norway. I do not have a job there yet but I am very confident I will get one as soon as I am there.

### Hemming — 2013-07-20T10:34:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-hungry.7211/post-90424>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=90422)

But I do not eat that.. so if your protein source is good I think no need to worry. Just try to make your main protein source from the sea, and it will only do you good.
>

> Finland is pretty expensive with foods. The prices has gone up 20% last years... that is pretty much! And the payments for work is pretty much constant. The only thing I can afford is.. food..lol That is why I am leaving to work in Norway. I do not have a job there yet but I am very confident I will get one as soon as I am there.

I eat a very strict diet biased towards seafood. I don't eat a lot of meat but prefer offal if I'm not eating seafood.

I do eat some pasteurised goat and sheep cheeses because I know where they have been made and that the production is organic. I only eat unpasteurised cow milk's cheeses - I completely agree regarding the taste

Your post just made the morning a lot better! Now I'm off for a swim in the ocean, some reading on the beach and to pick up some wild caught salmon at the harbour.

Have a great day too!

Is it ok to use PI2 potato extract and/or African mango for saiety issues while on the LeptinRx?

### Dali Dula — 2013-05-31T14:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87182>

You should not need any appetite supressants if you eat enough protein/fat.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-01T00:45:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87228>

Okay so what about colostrum? I have chronic constipation and overwhelming hunger and about 4%bf left to loose. I was breast fed btw.

### caroline — 2013-06-01T06:53:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87234>

> [LRichard12 said:](/goto/post?id=87228)
>
> Okay so what about colostrum? I have chronic constipation and overwhelming hunger and about 4%bf left to loose. I was breast fed btw.

Are you eating a big enough BAB? I needed about 80gr when I started. Add lots more fat as well.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-01T09:28:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87240>

Caroline,

I am eating 6 eggs, 120 grams of salmon, and plenty of goose fat, Ghee, and/or coconut oil for breakfast (about 2 tbls). It comes out to around 7 grams of protein and 66 grams of fat. I could up that but I really dont want to start gaining weight.

### caroline — 2013-06-01T10:44:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87241>

Sorry - but I don't understand how you are figuring out your protein?
Are you male or female? sometimes women gain a bit on the reset but weight loss at this point isn't the priority.
I weighed around 62K when I started the reset and did go up a bit but in a few months I went down two sizes......so my body comp was much better.

If you eat a big enough BAB your cravings will disappear and a lot of us shifted down to two meals a day pretty quickly.
Do you eat within 30 min of rising?
are you observing the absolutely no snacking rule?
At least 4 hours between meals?
Last meal at least 4 hours before bed?

All the details are hugely important

I feel like I am not being much help to you..... maybe post what you are eating in a day for us ..... that would be helpful.......

You really do have to eat a big enough BAB to be full - a lot of us had to stuff it down but it is hugely important.

You said something about 2 tbsp of fat - is that all?
Also - often seafood isn't filling enough for breakfast - maybe add in some grassfed beef?

### Dali Dula — 2013-06-01T14:50:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87247>

Caroline has good advice. More fat will not make you fat if you follow the protocols. Lots of coconut oil and magnesium will help with constipation.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-06-01T20:52:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87268>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=87247)
>
> Caroline has good advice. More fat will not make you fat if you follow the protocols. Lots of coconut oil and magnesium will help with constipation.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-02T04:13:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87300>

Colostrum is fine......but without destroying you hunger with fat your wasting your time.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-04T13:04:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87436>

Thank you Dr. Kruse,

This morning I ate 6 eggs, 2 white fish (Alaskan Pollock), about a Tbls each of Ghee and Goose fat, and about 1/4 of a jar of coconut oil (organic extra virgin). I'm off and on with my saiety so far this morning. I go from feeling satisfied to slightly hungry. I still have little energy and motivation today; it is really hard to get any work done. Though I took a couple of minutes worth of a cold shower this morning which immediately energised me. I came out of the shower on cloud nine! I plan on beginning your CT after workout this evening.

Any recommendation as to the amount and type of fat to consume with my BAB?

Would there be any real benifits to taking something like leptitrim?

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-04T13:05:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87437>

Thank you GlenPDQ! Could you post a link to that blog article for me? Thank you!

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-04T13:14:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87438>

Caroline,

I use Fitday PC to figure my macros. I'm a male, 22, 6foot, 155lbs.

I do eat within 30 min of rising, never snack (only coffee and organic decaf coffee-black no sugar no cream no sweeteners).

I usually wait about 5 hours between meals, and yes I eat dinner at 6pm every day and am in bed by 10:30-11.

I was adding 2 tbls of fat in addition to my 6 eggs and salmon (which both have fat). However, today I gorged on coconut oil, some ghee, and goose fat along with my eggs and fish. Still barely satisfied, though more so than I was before. No energy though.

Thanks for your help!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-04T17:12:02+0300

> [LRichard12 said:](/goto/post?id=87437)
>
> Thank you GlenPDQ! Could you post a link to that blog article for me? Thank you!

[http://www.jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://www.jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-04T17:12:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87449>

titrate fats until hunger goes away.......its simple.

### caroline — 2013-06-05T02:38:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87477>

Honestly - I would try lots of grass fed beef for Breakfast. Could you be sensitive to eggs? I could easily have eaten 6 eggs for breakfast and not been terribly full. Maybe switch your breakfast around for awhile - have a couple of GF hamburgers - cooked in lots of fat and then topped with CO - and maybe some bacon and avocado and then a couple of BPC and you should be all set. But - as Dr. K. said keep titrating the fat up ..... I will sometimes eat a slab of butter!
Please let us know how you do....

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-05T17:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87556>

Thank you Caroline! I will give it a try with the GF hamburgers instead of eggs.

Yesterday after lunch I expereinced for the first time in a while being full! It came after gorging on CO, Ghee, Goose fat, Macadamia nuts, and a couple of eggs. By dinner time I wasn't hungry for dinner, though I ate because someone took the time to prepare a low carb coconut cake for me, and I didn't want to be rude.

I had to skip breakfast today because I was scheduled for some fasting blood work (Cortisol, Leptin, A1c, Insulin, etc) at 9:45am. So I fasted until lunch which was not very hard at all. I wasn't really all that hungry until I started eating lunch, which triggered my hunger. Strangely though after dinner I was hungry again, even though I drenched my salad in a lot of olive oil, had roughly 2 ounces of macadamia nuts, an avacado, and a nice slab of salmon. Not sure what the deal is.

Will keep on with the fats as it seems to be working.

Thanks for your help!

### nonchalant — 2013-06-05T17:40:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87558>

LRichard, give it some time. I think that when the body finds a new source of good nutrition, it goes nuts for a while, gorging like a starving person would do.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-06T16:47:00+0300

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=87558)
>
> LRichard, give it some time. I think that when the body finds a new source of good nutrition, it goes nuts for a while, gorging like a starving person would do.

Thank you for the encouragement. Today I'm back to being hungry, even after gorging on olive oil, eggs, macadamia nuts, heavy cream, and almond butter (only a little bit). Will continue to up the fats. I've also upped my DHA/EPA uptake to about 3000 mg DHA.

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-06T16:48:37+0300

Dr. K I'm still constipated even after taking 3000mg of mag, a ton of probiotics, FOS, digestive enzymes, 3000mg DHA, etc. Any other suggestions?

### LRichard12 — 2013-06-10T18:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pi2-on-leptinrx.7212/post-87926>

Dear Caroline,

Thank you for your suggestion of egg intolerance. I had a food intolerance test done (cost €100.00, but well worth it I think) and found out that I am allergic to eggs, almonds, brazil nuts, and somewhat to wheat gluten, and dairy. On a scale of 1-5 eggs and almonds (2 staples in my diet) registered at a 4. So I've given up on eggs and almonds. Will now be doing slamon and coconut oil for my breakfast.

I also got some results back from my blood work today. Fasting cortisol was elevated (695), and the nurse said that my thyroid test was abnormal. Will see the doctor tomorrow.

I think my suspicion of adrenal fatigue could be correct.
